ENGG*2800.description: This second course in the design spine sequence emphasizes the environmental and social dimensions of sustainable design. Students will explore design philosophies and approaches in the context of the built environment. They will also examine Indigenous perspectives on sustainability and different ways of knowing, doing and being. Students will experience a community-focused design project which will provide the opportunity to develop oral communication, team, and project management skills. Computer use in design will include AutoCAD, ArcGIS, and SimaPro (or other similar software tools).
ENGG*2800.restriction: Registration in the CIVIL Engineering Major

ENGG*2820.title: Material Science for Civil Engineers
ENGG*2820.description: The course explores the properties of solids as a basis for material selection and design in diverse civil engineering applications. Atomic structures and arrangements that gives rise to material properties and failure mechanisms are examined. Behaviour in use such as durability, corrosion, deformation and fracture of materials are considered. Materials covered include metals and alloys, polymers, ceramics, glass, and composites. Emphasis is placed on materials commonly used in construction such as steel, wood, concrete and geotextiles. The environmental implications (e.g. material recovery, greenhouse gas emission) of material selection are explored. Emerging alternatives to enhance the sustainability and resilience of infrastructure are introduced.
ENGG*2820.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*2910.title: Mathematics for Artificial Intelligence
ENGG*2910.description: This course explores how mathematics and computation are unified for reasoning about data and making discoveries about the world. The emphasis is on fundamentals of mathematics required for AI and machine learning. Topics include computational methods for solving systems of linear equations including matrix decompositions, analytic geometry, solutions of nonlinear equations, functions of two or more variables, partial derivatives, and gradient-based optimization including second-order methods.
ENGG*2910.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3000.title: Engineering for Inclusion: Addressing Systemic Issues in Design and Engineering
ENGG*3000.description: This course is designed for engineering students who are committed to challenging and dismantling systemic issues within their profession. By fostering critical thinking, reflective decision-making, and effective action, this course empowers students to become inclusive engineers who can drive meaningful social change. Throughout the course, students will engage in interactive discussions and collaborate with members of the local community who will provide valuable insights and lead discussions on specific issues in the design process and engineering field. The course covers a range of pertinent subjects, including racism, intersectionality, Indigenous issues, gender-based discrimination, and more.

ENGG*3010.title: Introduction to Bioprocess Engineering
ENGG*3010.description: This course deals with principles of Bioprocess Engineering. Topics including enzyme process kinetics, immobilized enzyme systems, metabolic pathways, stoichiometry of microbial growth, bioreactor scale- up, operation and control, batch and continuous bioreactor operations, product recovery and purification.
ENGG*3010.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3020.title: Heat and Mass Transfer in Biological and Bioenvironmental Systems
ENGG*3020.description: This course deals with the basic concepts and application of heat and mass transfer in biological and bioenvironmental systems. Heat transfer topics including steady and unsteady state heat transfer, convective and heat transfer with change of phase. Mass transfer topics include steady and unsteady state mass transfer, convective mass transfer, gas-liquid mass transfer, and membrane transport processes.
ENGG*3020.restriction: ENGG*3160, ENGG*3430. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3040.title: Mechatronic Systems Design I
ENGG*3040.description: This course introduces the design of mechatronics systems, which are synergistic, combinations of components and controls drawn from mechanical engineering, electronics, and computer engineering. Topics include micro-electromechanical systems, smart materials, modern actuators, and multi-agent mechatronic systems, and mechatronics for manufacturing.
ENGG*3040.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3050.title: Embedded Reconfigurable Computing Systems
ENGG*3050.description: This course introduces the students to the analysis, synthesis and design of embedded systems and the implementation of embedded systems using Field Programmable Gate Arrays. Topics include: review of digital design concepts; Programmable Logic Devices; Field Programmable Logic Devices; physical design automation (partitioning, placement and routing); Hardware Descriptive Languages; VHDL; Verilog; High Level Languages; System-C; Handle-C; Fixed Point and Floating Point Arithmetic; Hardware Accelerators; Reconfigurable Instruction Set Computers; Hardware Software Co-design techniques; Application of Field Programmable Logic in Embedded Systems.
ENGG*3050.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the CENG specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3050.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3060.title: Machine Elements
ENGG*3060.description: This course covers concepts, procedures, and analysis techniques necessary to design mechanical elements that are part of mechatronic devices. Structural design against static and fatigue is covered. Component design includes screws, fasteners, shafts, bearings, flexible couplings and gears. Selection of motors and motion control will also be covered.
ENGG*3060.restriction: ENGG*3280. This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3070.title: Integrated Manufacturing Systems
ENGG*3070.description: Common production machines and manufacturing systems are dealt with, particularly automated systems, robotics, computer control and integration techniques, materials handling, inspection processes and process control. The course addresses societal and environmental issues related to manufacturing.
ENGG*3070.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3080.title: Energy Resources and Technologies
ENGG*3080.description: The challenges of changing the global energy system to reduce dependence on finite fossil energy sources, and transition to environmentally sustainable energy sources, are examined. The reserves, consumption, applications and environmental and human impacts of oil, coal and natural gas usage are examined. The fundamental principles, applications and status of a range of renewable energy sources and technologies will be covered to provide a solid background for further study of sustainable energy.
ENGG*3080.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3090.title: Digital Systems and Microcontroller Interfacing
ENGG*3090.description: This course focuses on the principles and techniques of modern digital systems and microcontroller interfacing. The course covers fundamental principles of combinational logic circuits, sequential logic circuits, integrated circuit logic families, arithmetic and MSI logic circuits, interfacing with the analog world including sensors and actuators, modern microcontroller basic architectures, instruction set architecture, and common interfacing microcontroller modules. The course introduces practical interfacing applications with microcontrollers, including serial and parallel interfacing and analog input/output interfacing.
ENGG*3090.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3100.title: Engineering and Design III
ENGG*3100.description: This course is the third in the sequence of engineering and design courses. Design teams (frequently crossing engineering disciplines) define a problem of modest complexity, with attention to the benefits and risks to users, the environment and society. Students learn and apply methods for idea generation, evaluation of design alternatives and refinement of solutions, which may include a preliminary simulation or software analysis. Technical writing emphasizes revision for clarity and cohesion. Students learn to develop evidence-based arguments and apply this to defend design solutions in terms of primary function, safety, economics, and environmental and societal risks and benefits. Team skills including leadership, interdisciplinary collaboration and conflict resolution are further developed. Project management, in the context of bringing together time, money and people, is practiced. Students reflect and receive feedback on their design process to foster growth in areas including lifelong learning, teamwork, project management and professional behaviour.

prerequisites.raw: Completion of 6.00 credits of ENGG courses including ENGG*2100

ENGG*3100.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, School of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*3120.title: Computer Aided Design and Manufacturing
ENGG*3120.description: The course presents the elements of solid modelling, creation of parts of increasing complexity and the assembly of parts to form a final design, along with mechanism simulation. The operation and programming of CNC machines is covered.
ENGG*3120.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3130.title: Modelling Complex Systems
ENGG*3130.description: This course explores the application of systems thinking to complex global issues. Key topics will include: systems theory, complex adaptive systems, systems tools, and systems approaches. The course will emphasize the role of computational modelling and simulation as a central tool for applying systems thinking to real-world settings.
ENGG*3130.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the ESC specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3140.title: Mechanical Vibration
ENGG*3140.description: This course will provide students with an introduction to the fundamental concepts of vibration engineering using both single and multiple degrees of freedom concepts. The free and forced response of these systems will be covered. Emphasis will be placed on the design of vibration suppression and isolation of mechanical systems. Concepts of natural frequencies and mode shapes and their significance in the solution of multiple degrees of freedom problems will be covered. Vibration of rotating machinery, balancing, condition monitoring, and predictive vs. preventative maintenance philosophies will be introduced.
ENGG*3140.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3150.title: Engineering Biomechanics
ENGG*3150.description: The following topics related to biomechanics are covered in this course: kinematic and kinetic analysis techniques; electromyography; current techniques in laboratory instrumentation and biomedical applications.
ENGG*3150.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3170.title: Biomaterials
ENGG*3170.description: Physical properties of natural and synthetic (e.g. stainless steel, polymers) materials used in biological engineering applications are presented in this course. Topics will include microstructure and mechanical properties of typical biomaterials, quantification of advanced material properties and behaviours, fabrication, compatibility, biodegradation and mechanical failure. Typical applications will include processing of biomaterials as well as equipment and implant design.
ENGG*3170.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BIOE and BME specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3180.title: Air Quality
ENGG*3180.description: The study of the transport, transformation and deposition processes associated with air pollutants. The chemical and biological nature, impacts, and sources of air pollutants. The physical aspects of the atmospheric boundary layer. The mathematical treatment of diffusion in a homogeneous field in a boundary layer. Regulatory approaches worldwide and their use of air quality modeling. The use of models for the design of stacks and monitoring networks.
ENGG*3180.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the ENVE specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3180.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3210.title: Communication Systems
ENGG*3210.description: This course is an introduction to the fundamentals of data communication and computer networking. The data communication basics will cover signal transmission and signal encoding techniques such as: multiplexing techniques, signaling, encoding and decoding, error detection and recovery, sliding window techniques. Computer networking basics will cover: communication network components and topologies, multiple access design issues and performance analysis, switching, routing, services and applications, and security. The course will also cover the mathematical tools (Fourier transform, etc.) used in signal analysis.
ENGG*3210.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3210.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3220.title: Groundwater Engineering
ENGG*3220.description: This course introduces water resources engineering and environmental engineering students to the fundamentals of groundwater systems. Emphasis is placed on quantitative analyses required for groundwater resource extraction and quality protection of the saturated zone. Laboratories emphasize problem solving, use of commercial software and practical groundwater engineering investigation.
ENGG*3220.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3220.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3240.title: Engineering Economics
ENGG*3240.description: This course covers the principles of project evaluation; analysis of capital and operating costs of engineering alternatives, benefit-cost ratio; break- even studies, evaluations recognizing risk, replacement and retirement of assets; tax considerations, influence of sources of funds.
ENGG*3240.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3250.title: Energy Management and Utilization
ENGG*3250.description: This course introduces notions of energy conservation and efficiency, an integrated approach to energy auditing and examples of typical applications (examples include: steam generation and distribution, process or comfort cooling, pumping and compressed air, human needs for modern living, energy consumption in buildings and industry). It also covers pinch technology and its application for energy recovery in industry, and methods to achieve low energy buildings.
ENGG*3250.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3260.title: Thermodynamics
ENGG*3260.description: This course covers macroscopic thermodynamics and its applications to engineering practice. Topics include properties of pure substances and equilibrium, the First Law of thermodynamics (energy transfer and energy balance in closed and flow systems), the Second Law of thermodynamics and its applications (entropy analysis of closed and flow systems, quantification of irreversibilities and inefficiencies, quality of energy, etc.), thermodynamic cycles and exergy.
ENGG*3260.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3260.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3270.title: Engineering Electromagnetics
ENGG*3270.description: This course explores the fundamentals of electromagnetics in electrical engineering. Course topics include the following: vectors and spatial geometry, partial derivatives, multiple integrals, vector calculus, electrostatics, steady-state electric circuits, magnetostatics, Ampere's Law, Faraday's Law, and propagation of electromagnetic waves. Applications within electrical engineering will be covered.
ENGG*3270.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3280.title: Machine Design
ENGG*3280.description: This course provides the concepts, procedures, and analysis techniques necessary to design various mechanical elements commonly found in machines. Failure analysis such as yield criteria and fatigue are covered. Component design includes screws, fasteners, shafts, bearings and lubrication, and gears. The emphasis is on the use of readily available materials, standard component, and appropriate design approaches to achieve safe and efficient system design.
ENGG*3280.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3340.title: Geographic Information Systems in Environmental Engineering
ENGG*3340.description: Geographical information system structure and functions. Data structuring and application program development. Data input, display and analysis. Applications in environmental engineering and natural resource development/management. Students will be able to use a GIS software package to build geographical information systems.

prerequisites.raw: (1 of CIS*1300, CIS*1500, ENGG*1410), (MATH*1080 or MATH*1200)

ENGG*3340.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the WRE specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3370.title: Applied Fluids and Thermodynamics
ENGG*3370.description: This course builds on the fundamentals of fluid dynamics and thermodynamics introduced in previous courses by looking at relevant applications. Topics to be covered include: heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC); heat engine systems such as the Carnot cycle for refrigeration and heat pumps and the Rankine cycle for vapour power systems; compressible flow, turbomachinery such as pumps, turbines, and propellers; and an introduction to combustion.
ENGG*3370.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the MECH specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3380.title: Computer Organization and Design
ENGG*3380.description: This course contains a detailed examination of modern computer organization and techniques for microprocessor architecture design. Topics include - CPU design; instruction set design, addressing modes, operands; data flow design: internal bus structure, data flow signals, registers; control sequence design: hardwired control, decoding, microprogramming; architecture classes: CISC, RISC, and DSP; Memory organization; performance. Students must complete a term project that includes design, implementation, and demonstration of a CPU using a hardware descriptive language like VHDL.
ENGG*3380.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the CENG specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3380.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3390.title: Signal Processing
ENGG*3390.description: This course will establish the fundamental analysis and design techniques for signal processing systems. Topics covered include: definition and properties of linear time-invariant systems; impulse response and convolution; continuous-time Laplace transform, Fourier series, Fourier transform; discrete-time Fourier transform, discrete-time Fourier series, fast Fourier transform, Z transform; complex frequency response; filter analysis and design for both continuous and discrete time systems. Students will be able to design continuous-time filters and both design and implement discrete-time digital filters using computer-based tools.
ENGG*3390.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BME, CENG and ESC specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3390.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3410.title: Systems and Control Theory
ENGG*3410.description: This course introduces the theory and techniques required to analyze and design of closed loop, automatic controllers for engineering systems, with applications in electrical, mechanical, and biomedical systems. Topics include modeling in time and frequency domains (including linear time invariance, causality, and linearization); sketching and interpreting Bode plots; stability of closed loop systems (including analysis via Routh Hurwitz and Nyquist); performance metrics including gain and phase margin, sensitivity, and error tracking; and control using ON/OFF, PID, root locus, and frequency domain (lead/lag) techniques. Computer tools to aid in control design and analysis are a crucial component of the course. Optional advanced topics include but are not limited to state-space control, performance limitations, and control of non-linear systems.
ENGG*3410.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BME, CENG, ESC and MECH specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3430.title: Heat and Mass Transfer
ENGG*3430.description: Analysis of steady and transient thermal systems involving heat transfer by conduction, convection and radiation and of mass transfer by molecular diffusion and convection. Other topics include the thermal analysis of heat exchangers and heat transfer systems involving a change of state.
ENGG*3430.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3440.title: Process Control
ENGG*3440.description: Students will learn about dynamic process behaviour and process control strategies relevant to biological and environmental engineering, including how to analyze, model, predict and control processes in time and frequency domains. Performance and stability parameters will be illustrated with root locus diagrams. Control by on/off and proportional (and its advanced variants) controllers will be applied to feedback control problems including regulating physical and chemical properties, and biological and chemical reactions. This course includes experimental process control laboratory work and use of controller modeling software.
ENGG*3440.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BIOE and ENVE specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3440.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3450.title: Electronic Devices
ENGG*3450.description: This course explores the theory and principles of modern electronic devices and their applications in circuits. Course topics include: intrinsic and doped semiconductors; drift and diffusion currents; metal-semiconductor contacts and MOS capacitors; pn junctions and breakdown phenomena; solid-state diodes; bipolar and MOS field- effect transistors; current-voltage characteristics and biasing; small- signal models and operation; circuit integration; analysis and design of application circuits, operational transconductance amplifiers, and logic gates.
ENGG*3450.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BIOE, BME, CENG and ESC specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3450.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3470.title: Mass Transfer Operations
ENGG*3470.description: Application of mass transfer principles in natural and engineered systems. Mass transport in the multi-media fate of contaminants in and between air, water and land. Design and analysis of separation processes for emission and pollutant prevention.
ENGG*3470.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3470.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3490.title: Introduction to Mechatronic Systems Design
ENGG*3490.description: This course covers the design of mechatronic systems, which are synergistic, combinations of components and controls drawn from mechanical engineering, electronics, and computer engineering. The course covers the following areas: (1) modeling of mechatronic systems (mechanical, electrical/electronic systems) and understanding their behaviour, (2) sensing and measurement including a variety of mechatronics sensors (fundamentals and applications), (3) actuators specific to mechatronics including motors and drivers (fundamentals and applications), (4) basic microcontroller programming as well as sensor/actuator integrations, and (5) control and its applications in mechatronics.
ENGG*3490.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3510.title: Electromechanical Devices
ENGG*3510.description: The aim of this course is to develop an understanding of the electrical and electromechanical principles and their applications as devices used in engineering. The course covers magnetic fields of currents and coils; magnetic materials; magnetic circuits; induced, electric and magnetic fields (EMF), inductance, transformers magnetic forces, permanent magnets and electromagnets. The course examines the principles of variable-reluctance devices, stepper motors, moving-coil devices, direct current (DC) and alternating current (AC) motors. Semiconductors materials and devices, diodes, and transistors; principles of modern electronic devices and their applications in circuits; as well as operational amplifiers and digital logics are also studied.
ENGG*3510.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3570.title: MEMS and Microfabrication
ENGG*3570.description: This course presents a broad survey of micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) and microfabrication technologies. It covers silicon and non-silicon microfabrication techniques for microsensors, microactuators, and nanotechnology. It introduces CAD tools and mechanical and electrical issues in designing devices such as micromotors, grippers, accelerometers, and pressure sensors. It discusses limitations and challenges in design and fabrication of MEMS and enables the application of general micromachining principles to developing novel devices.
ENGG*3570.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3590.title: Water Quality
ENGG*3590.description: This course builds on the student's experience in chemistry, biology, physics and fluid mechanics, and provides an engineering perspective on: (i) standard methods of water quality analysis for physical, chemical and biological characteristics of water; (ii) significance and interpretation of analytical results, (iii) modeling of water quality in natural systems and (iv) introduction to engineered water and wastewater treatment systems.

prerequisites.raw: ENGG*2230, STAT*2120, (BIOL*1090 or MICR*2420), (ENGG*2010 or ENGG*2560)

ENGG*3590.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the ENVE and WRE specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3590.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3600.title: Introduction to Thermal-Fluid Sciences
ENGG*3600.description: This course provides an introduction to fluid and thermal systems. Students will identify the properties of solids and fluids, and solve problems involving elementary statics, kinematics, and dynamics of fluids, including internal and external flows. Students will also apply the laws of thermodynamics and analyse energy transfers in a variety of systems. Students will apply their knowledge of thermodynamic cycles and analyze steady and transient thermal systems involving heat transfer by conduction, convection and radiation in practical systems.
ENGG*3600.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3640.title: Microcomputer Interfacing
ENGG*3640.description: This course focuses on the subject of interfacing microcomputers to external equipment. Topics include peripheral devices, hardware interfaces, device driver software and real time programming. Advanced programming: debugging of embedded systems, data structures and subroutine calls, high-level system programming. Interrupts and resets, real time events, signal generation and timing measurements. Synchronous and asynchronous serial communication. Parallel I/O ports and synchronization techniques. I/O interfacing, microcomputer busses, memory interfacing and direct memory access (DMA). Data acquisition topics include signal conditioning analog to digital conversion and digital signal processing.
ENGG*3640.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the CENG and ESC specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3640.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*3650.title: Hydrology and Hydraulics
ENGG*3650.description: This course provides a quantitative study of natural water circulation systems with emphasis on basic physical principles and interrelationships among major hydrologic and hydraulic processes in watersheds. The characteristics of mass and energy and inputs to and outputs from watersheds are explored. Factors governing precipitation occurrence, evaporation and infiltration rates, groundwater recharge and discharge, runoff generation, methods of streamflow analysis and open channel flow in rivers and streams are considered. Hydrologic field measurements, mathematical modeling of watershed processes and the effects of climate change on precipitation inputs and other watershed processes, are included.
ENGG*3650.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3650.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3670.title: Soil Mechanics and Site Characterization
ENGG*3670.description: This course focuses on the mechanics of soil materials applied to geotechnical engineering problems. It covers soil classification and the physical and chemical properties of soil that relate to its strength. It examines interactive forces in soil water systems and the role of groundwater in geotechnical engineering problems. Topics studied include: effective stress, consolidation, shear strength and lateral earth pressure. Theory is applied to geotechnical problems such as settlement and stability of earth slopes. Students will gain an understanding of approaches to site investigation and will perform testing in the field and laboratory to characterize soil properties. Students will have an opportunity to use software tools that support problem analysis and design.
ENGG*3670.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3670.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3680.title: Introduction to AI for Civil Engineering
ENGG*3680.description: This course provides the foundational understanding necessary to leverage artificial intelligence (AI) to address civil engineering challenges. It introduces AI concepts such as machine learning, neural networks, and data analytics, and explores their application in promoting sustainable and resilient engineering solutions. Topics include predictive maintenance for infrastructure, automation in construction, and smart city technologies. Students will develop skills in using AI for data-driven decision-making, risk assessment, and system optimization. Case studies will illustrate the transformative impact of AI on project efficiency, safety, and sustainability. They will explore opportunities to implement AI technologies to create resilient urban systems that efficiently manage resources and respond to changing conditions.
ENGG*3680.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3690.title: Electrical Power Systems
ENGG*3690.description: This course provides an introduction to fundamental modelling and analysis techniques used in electricity generation, transmission, and distribution, with an emphasis on nonlinear system analysis. Topics include functional descriptions and modelling of key components such as generators, transformers, transmission lines, motors, and other electrical loads. The course covers power flow analysis in depth, from basic equations to their application in power networks. Additionally, it introduces fault analysis and the principles of basic protection systems and electrical safety.

ENGG*3700.title: Optimization for Engineers
ENGG*3700.description: This course serves as an introduction to optimization. Topics to be covered include but are not limited to: linear programming, sensitivity analysis, linear integer programming technique, dynamic programming, Markov chains, transportation method, decision analysis, and queuing theory.
ENGG*3700.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*3700.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*3770.title: Physics of Livable Buildings
ENGG*3770.description: The course brings that knowledge and insight to enable the hygrothermal analysis and design of buildings. In the context of ongoing climate change, and the large share of the built environment in it, the hygrothermal performance of buildings is becoming increasingly important. Knowledge of and insight into the transport of heat, air and moisture in buildings, building components and building materials is therefore of great importance for structural engineers and engineer- architects. The use of sensors for continuous monitoring and active control, the response of materials to hydrothermal cycles, the role of heat and energy management strategies on hygrothermal performance, and the human health and comfort impacts of hygrothermal conditions will be considered.

prerequisites.raw: CHEM*1050, MATH*2270, (1 of ENGG*2120 or ENGG*2820)

ENGG*3770.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3780.title: Communication Circuits
ENGG*3780.description: This course covers the analog and digital circuits required to generate and detect the transmitted signals and transform these into useful information. Although almost all communication signals are now processed digitally, the transmission and reception of these is an analog process whether this is wireless, wired, or optical. The course will discuss the major blocks in a communication system and examine the specification and detailed circuit implementation of some of these blocks.

ENGG*3820.title: Sustainable Materials in Civil Engineering
ENGG*3820.description: This course further examines materials used in civil engineering applications, with an emphasis on approaches to improve the sustainability of traditional materials as well as emerging alternatives. Students will be encouraged to think holistically about the economic, environmental and social implications of material selection. The course is intended to complement the content of structural analysis and design courses by providing students with the opportunity to prepare, test, and build with various materials and by extending to other applications such as pavement systems. Reinforcement and control of cracking in concrete systems will be examined. Students will learn about the use of carbon sequestering concrete and recovered materials. Approaches to extend the life of materials, self-healing materials, and materials used to rehabilitate infrastructure will also be explored.
ENGG*3820.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3880.title: Field Methods in Civil, Environmental and Water Resources Engineering
ENGG*3880.description: This course explores field methods and practical approaches in civil, environmental and water resources engineering. Topics related to land development and construction, hydrogeology and geotechnical engineering, air quality, streamflow hydraulics and hydrology, and water/ wastewater services are explored in a hands-on experiential learning environment. Indigenous ways of knowing and doing are explored through land-based learning. Professionalism and communication skills are developed through learning experiences in the course.
ENGG*3880.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3910.title: Structural Analysis
ENGG*3910.description: This course provides an introduction to the fundamentals of structural analysis, which focuses on understanding the response of structures to external loads and internal forces. The course will emphasize problem analysis. Students will begin with analysis of statically determinate structures. They will then learn to use force and displacement methods for analysis of statically indeterminate structures. The course includes slope-deflection equations, moment distribution and stiffness methods. Students will gain familiarity with structural analysis software tools.
ENGG*3910.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3920.title: Structural Design
ENGG*3920.description: This course introduces methodologies for structural design and provides fundamentals for the design of wood, steel and concrete structures at the element and system levels. Students will learn to determine applied loads and calculate the distribution of forces in structural systems. They will size and analyze tension and compression members and beams. Students will become familiar with the standards/codes used for structural design in Canada and will apply software tools to support structural design and analysis.
ENGG*3920.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3950.title: Sustainable Transportation Systems
ENGG*3950.description: The course will examine the characteristics of various transportation systems and the current and future influences on and demands for these systems. It will introduce traffic flow theory and approaches to improve transportation system performance, with an emphasis on networks in congested urban areas. Simple quantitative models will be used to develop an understanding of the factors affecting traffic safety and the factors influencing noise and air quality emissions. Students will also explore the social dimensions of urban and regional mobility. Students will identify and learn about emerging transportation issues and technologies.
ENGG*3950.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*3960.title: Machine Design
ENGG*3960.description: Students will learn to design and fabricate a machine. In groups, they will design and select components, fabricate and procure parts, and assemble a machine. Emphasis is on the technical basis for the design, component selection, design communication; and function, fit, and finish of the assembled machine.[Department(s):Department of Mechanical Engineering]

ENGG*4000.title: Proposal for Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4000.description: In this course students will prepare a proposal for the design project that will be completed in the Engineering Design IV course in their program of study. Teams normally of 3 to 4 students (single student groups not allowed) will prepare the proposal, providing details on the proposed project, identify the group's members and identify the faculty adviser, who has a P.Eng.. Students are responsible for creating their own design group and securing a faculty advisor.
ENGG*4000.restriction: Registration in the semester preceding registration in the capstone ENGG*41x0 Engineering Design IV course. Instructor consent required. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, School of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4010.title: Unit Operations
ENGG*4010.description: This course deals with the concepts of unit operations in biochemical engineering. Analysis and design of separation processes including drying, humidification, adsorption, leaching, membrane separation and centrifugation will be discussed. Topics including fluid properties, flow and dynamics will also be covered.

prerequisites.raw: (ENGG*2230 or FOOD*2620), (1 of ENGG*3020, ENGG*3430, FOOD*3170)

ENGG*4010.restriction: ENGG*3830. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4020.title: Engineering Sustainability in Food and Agriculture
ENGG*4020.description: This course deals with the process and practices associated with food production and processing globally and in Canada. An insight into the rapidly changing context of the food system, its achievements, vulnerabilities, and innovation for a sustainable food future will be provided. Students will work in groups to develop engineering-based solutions for improvement of the current and future food and agricultural practices.
ENGG*4020.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4030.title: Manufacturing System Design
ENGG*4030.description: Students work in groups to design a manufacturing system to produce a specific product. Choices must be made about the materials to be used, the methods to manufacture each part of the product and the final assembly and/or packaging process. Attention is paid to economics and efficiency of the overall manufacturing system.
ENGG*4030.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4040.title: Medical Imaging Modalities
ENGG*4040.description: The course will cover the basic knowledge of medical imaging systems, how they operate and to what uses they can be applied. Systems covered will include x-ray radiography, computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, positron emission tomography, gama cameras, and ultrasound imaging. Emphasis will be on the underlying physics and computation, highlighting factors affecting image quality, patient safety, and clinical use.
ENGG*4040.restriction: PHYS*4070. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4050.title: Quality Control
ENGG*4050.description: The basic techniques and regulations surrounding quality control in a generic manufacturing environment are covered. The topics covered include: total quality management including relevant ISO regulations, six sigma, reliability, statistical process control, acceptance sampling and 2k factorial design of experiments.
ENGG*4050.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4060.title: Biomedical Signals Processing
ENGG*4060.description: This course will cover the generation of biomedical signals, detection and measurement, and processing. The physiology of electrical signal generation will cover ionic transport in cellular membranes and propagation of electrical signals in cells and tissues. The range of biomedical signals covered includes such common signals as the electromyogram (EMG), the electrocardiogram (ECG), the electroencephalogram (EEG). Detection and measurement will cover electrode technology, instrumentation amplifiers and safety concerns. Processing includes filtering, frequency content analysis, removal of artifacts, signal correlation, and event detection.
ENGG*4060.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4070.title: Life Cycle Assessment for Sustainable Design
ENGG*4070.description: This course will introduce students to the fundamental concepts related to interaction of industrial and environmental/ecological systems, sustainability challenges facing the current generation, and systems- based approaches required to create sustainable solutions for society. Students will understand the concepts and the scientific method as it applies to a systems-based, transdisciplinary approach to sustainability, and will be prepared to identify problems in sustainability and formulate appropriate solutions based on scientific research, applied science, social and economic issues. The basic concepts of life cycle assessment (LCA) will be discussed, along with life cycle inventory (LCI) and life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) including the social and economic dimensions. The application of life cycle assessment methodology using appropriate case studies will be presented.
ENGG*4070.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4070.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4080.title: Micro and Nano-Scale Electronics
ENGG*4080.description: The purpose of this course is to describe the operating principles of analog integrated micro and nano electronic circuits and to teach how to design and use such circuits systems. Course topics include: device and circuit fabrication in silicon and non-silicon based technologies; operation and layout of active and passive elements; analog and switched-capacitor filters; analog-to-digital and digital-toanalog converters; amplifiers; oscillators and circuits for radio-frequency and optical communications; readout channels for integrated sensors, and analog integrated circuits for mechatronics and bioengineering. The main emphasis is on device models, circuit operation, and design techniques.
ENGG*4080.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4080.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4090.title: Food and Beverage Engineering
ENGG*4090.description: Students work in groups to design a system for manufacturing a specific food or beverage product. Choices are made about the specific processes to be used, the final packaging and marketing of the product. Attention is paid to the economics and efficiency of the overall production process.
ENGG*4090.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4101.title: Electrical Engineering and Design IV
ENGG*4101.description: This course serves as the first phase of the capstone design experience for the Electrical Engineering program. Students will enhance their competencies in professionalism, ethics, and equity through the analysis of diverse Electrical Engineering case studies. Key topics include tools and methodologies for the design, implementation, and management of electrical systems, as well as project planning, risk assessment, and system integration. As part of the course, students will form multidisciplinary teams (typically four members) to prepare a comprehensive proposal for a design project to be completed in the subsequent term. The proposal must clearly define the design problem, addressing safety, environmental, social, and economic considerations, and outline a detailed work plan. Teams are also responsible for securing a faculty advisor with a P.Eng. designation. This course emphasizes teamwork, practical problem-solving, and the application of engineering principles in real-world scenarios, laying the groundwork for the final design project.
ENGG*4101.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4102. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, School of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4102.title: Electrical Engineering and Design IV
ENGG*4102.description: This is the second half of the capstone design course for the Electrical Engineering program. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Design teams will continue through the design process to prepare a fully specified solution, at the level of preliminary or final design, to the problem defined in the first half of the course. Through a final report and presentation, design teams provide an evidence-based defense of their design, demonstrating its functionality and performance while addressing key considerations such as safety, reliability, and sustainability (economic, environmental, and social).

ENGG*4110.title: Biological Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4110.description: This is the capstone design course for the Biological Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a problem in a biological system or process. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.BIOE core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4110.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4110. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4120.title: Engineering Systems and Computing Design IV
ENGG*4120.description: This is the capstone design course for the Engineering Systems and Computing program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a problem involving control system, computer hardware or computer software technology. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.ESC core courses, ENGG*4000

ENGG*4120.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4120. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4130.title: Environmental Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4130.description: This is the capstone design course for the Environmental Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to an environmental engineering problem. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.ENVE core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4130.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4130. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4150.title: Water Resources Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4150.description: This is the capstone design course for the Water Resources Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a problem involving water resources or wastewater engineering. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.WRE core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4150.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4150. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4160.title: Mechanical Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4160.description: This is the capstone design course for the Mechanical Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a mechanical engineering problem. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.MECH core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4160.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4160. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4170.title: Computer Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4170.description: This is the capstone design course for the Computer Engineering program. Teams of normally 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a computer engineering problem. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.CENG core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4170.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4170. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4180.title: Biomedical Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4180.description: This is the capstone design course for the Biomedical Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a biomedical engineering problem. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.BME core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4180.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4180. Maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4190.title: Mechatronics Engineering Design IV
ENGG*4190.description: This is the capstone design course for the Mechatronics Engineering program. Teams normally of 3-4 students apply engineering analysis and design principles to a mechatronics engineering problem. A completely specified solution at the level of preliminary or final design is required, including assessment of socio-economic and environmental impact. This is a small group design that requires reports and a poster presentation to a professional standard. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Only students currently registered in ENGG*4000 will be permitted to select this course.

prerequisites.raw: 19.75 credits towards the BENG MTE major, all 1000 and 2000 level core courses, ENGG*4000.

ENGG*4190.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Restricted to students with a maximum of 3.75 credits remaining in their BENG degree, including ENGG*4190. A maximum semester credit load of 3.25 credits. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, College of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4200.title: Wireless Sensor Networks
ENGG*4200.description: This course focuses on the fundamentals behind the design of wireless sensor networks. Topics include node architecture, operating systems, prototypes and applications for wireless sensor networks. The course emphasizes basic architectural framework including physical layer, medium access control layer and network layer. It also covers network management topics such as power management, time synchronization and localization. The course has a number of experiments with sensor network software and hardware. The primary focus of the experiments is to give students hands-on programming experience with various microcontrollers and sensing platforms.
ENGG*4200.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4200.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4210.title: Machine Learning
ENGG*4210.description: The aim of this course is to provide students with an introduction to algorithms and techniques of machine learning, particularly in engineering applications. The emphasis will be on the fundamentals, and not a specific approach or software tool. The course will cover and compare current popular approaches and their applicability to various engineering problems, and will provide hands-on experience with some of the tools.
ENGG*4210.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4220.title: Interdisciplinary Mechanical Engineering Design
ENGG*4220.description: This is a general design course for students registered in the B. Eng. major in mechanical engineering who wish to develop a broad based mechanical engineering foundation. Students work in groups to develop a general mechanical engineering design. Special attention is paid to the sustainability of the design, its economic feasibility and overall efficiency.
ENGG*4220.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4230.title: Energy Conversion
ENGG*4230.description: The course introduces the technical criteria for the design of efficient energy conversion processes and systems. It includes the review of boilers and cycles, fuel and combustion calculations, and fundamentals of both traditional and emerging energy conversion processes and systems for production of thermal, mechanical, and electrical energy. Topics include fossil, biomass and nuclear fuels, as well as wind, solar, geothermal energy and fuel cells. Mechanisms for storing energy generated from each of these are also studied. The course also explores conversion of automobile systems, renovation of old fossil fuel fired plant technology, co-firing of opportunity fuels, waste to energy technology, emissions, and economics of energy projects.
ENGG*4230.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4240.title: Site Remediation
ENGG*4240.description: Remediation of contaminated sites is done to mitigate impacts to the environment and public health. The course will: review the applicable legislation; identify the important soil, water, air and chemical interactions; review the steps of an environmental risk assessment so that contaminated sites can be identified and evaluated to see if remediation is required; and evaluate and appraise various remediation technologies to complete the soil and groundwater remediation.
ENGG*4240.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4240.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4250.title: River Hydraulic Design for Sustainability and Resiliency
ENGG*4250.description: This course discusses methods of stream flow characterization for design of hydraulic structures, river restoration, flood warning, flood plain mapping and water management. Hydraulic analysis is applied to the design of dams, control structures, energy dissipation structures, bridges and culverts. Hydraulic modeling and analyses of steady flow profiles, flood waves, and sediment transport are applied in the design of natural and constructed channels and protective works for rivers to achieve social, ecological and economic sustainability and greater resiliency to the effects of climate change.
ENGG*4250.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4250.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4300.title: Food Processing Engineering Design
ENGG*4300.description: This course covers the design of various food processing operations and the physico-chemical and mechano-biological response of foods to such operations. Topics include: process evaluation; optimization of operations such as thermal and chemical processes; and rheological properties of various food forms.
ENGG*4300.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4340.title: Solid and Hazardous Waste Management
ENGG*4340.description: Solid waste generation rates and waste composition. Integrated waste management: collection, recovery, reuse, recycling, energy-from-waste, and landfilling. Biological treatment of the organic waste fraction - direct land application, composting, anaerobic digestion. Environmental impact of waste management and sustainable development. Cross media issues related to solid waste disposal. An introduction to hazardous waste management and treatment methods.
ENGG*4340.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the ENVE specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4340.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4360.title: Conservation and Restoration in Rural Watersheds
ENGG*4360.description: The course discusses the properties of soils and land use governing the occurrence and magnitude of overland flow, infiltration, percolation of soil water, and variations in soil water storage. Students will learn to design surface and subsurface drainage systems for rural land. Students will design soil and water management systems and structures to control soil erosion and protect surface water and groundwater quality. The course explores nitrogen and phosphorus cycles and design of Best Management Practices (BMPs) under changing land use and climate conditions.
ENGG*4360.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4360.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4370.title: Urban Water Systems Design
ENGG*4370.description: Estimation of water quantity and quality needed for urban water supply and drainage. Design of water supply, pumping systems, pipe networks and distributed storage reservoirs from analysis of steady and transient, pressurized and free surface flow. Rates of generation of flows and pollutants to sanitary and storm sewers, design of buried pipe and open channel drainage systems with structures for flow and pollution control. Modeling of water systems for sustainable urban development.
ENGG*4370.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4370.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4380.title: Bioreactor Design
ENGG*4380.description: Topics in this course include: modeling and design of batch and continuous bioreactors based on biological growth kinetics and mass balances; gas-liquid mass transfer for aeration and agitation; instrumentation; and control.
ENGG*4380.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BIOE specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4390.title: Bio-instrumentation Design
ENGG*4390.description: Theory and selection criteria of devices used in measurements in biological systems; design of complete measurement systems including transducers, signal conditioning and recording components; error analysis. Differences between measurements in biological and physical systems.
ENGG*4390.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BIOE and BME specializations in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4400.title: Biomechanical Engineering Design
ENGG*4400.description: This course covers concept development, design, modeling, manufacture and testing of biomechanical devices including athletic equipment, assistive devices, medical implants and tools. Other topics include the biomechanical factors influencing design, regulatory issues, current development trends, and the possible future direction of design and technology.
ENGG*4400.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4420.title: Real-time Systems Design
ENGG*4420.description: This course teaches real-time concepts from a system and computing perspective covering topics related to four major areas. Real-time computer control and system modeling area teaches basic real-time design and system modeling concepts for hard and soft real-time computer control applications. Real-time Operating Systems (RTOS) area introduces common kernel objects and inter-task communication and synchronization using examples from current commercial RTOS. Topics in the area of scheduling present theoretical results related to uniprocessor and multiprocessor scheduling algorithms and topics in the area of fault tolerance and reliability present current techniques at software and hardware level.
ENGG*4420.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4420.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4430.title: Neuro-Fuzzy and Soft Computing Systems
ENGG*4430.description: This course covers the basics of fuzzy systems, neural networks and neuro-fuzzy systems. The main focus is the concepts and algorithms of fuzzy sets, rules, and reasoning, as well as neural network structures, supervised learning and unsupervised learning of neural networks, and hybrid neuro-fuzzy systems. The applications of neural networks and fuzzy systems to control systems, signal processing, systems modeling and systems identification will be presented through examples.
ENGG*4430.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4430.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4440.title: Computational Fluid Dynamics
ENGG*4440.description: Computational methods for fluid mechanics form the core of the course. The concepts of modelling are covered including numerical analysis, the governing equations for fluid problems and finite discretization methods. Mathematical models for turbulence are presented and the student is exposed to the use of commercial software for the solution of complex problems in fluid dynamics.
ENGG*4440.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4450.title: Large-Scale Software Architecture Engineering
ENGG*4450.description: This course introduces the students to the analysis, synthesis and design of large-scale software systems at the architectural level. This is in contrast to the algorithmic and data structure viewpoint of most software systems. Large-scale software systems are complex, execute on many processors, under different operating systems, use a particular or many language(s) of implementation, and typically rely on system layers, network connectivity, messaging and data management and hardware interfacing. The material covered includes architectural styles, case studies, architectural design techniques, formal models, specifications and architectural design tools. The laboratory sessions will expose the students to analyzing and redesigning an existing large-scale software system.
ENGG*4450.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4450.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4460.title: Robotic Systems
ENGG*4460.description: This course covers robot technology fundamentals, mathematical representation of kinematics, planning and execution of robot trajectories, introduction to robot languages, programming of robotic systems, different application domains for robots (e.g. assembly, manufacturing, medical, services, etc.), and robot sensors. The goal of this course is to provide students with a comprehensive background, approaches and skills to apply robotics technology to real world engineering applications and problems.
ENGG*4460.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4470.title: Finite Element Analysis
ENGG*4470.description: The theory of finite element analysis is presented including element derivation and solution procedures. Students use a finite element package to solve problems based on static and dynamic applications in mechanical systems. Examples are chosen from classical machines as well as biological systems.
ENGG*4470.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4480.title: Advanced Mechatronic Systems Design
ENGG*4480.description: The course is a follow up to the introductory mechatronics course and aims at covering advanced topics that are necessary in developing mechatronic systems. Topics include: signal conditioning and filtering for mechatronics system including advanced filters such as Kalman filters; important/advanced electronic circuits for mechatronics systems; microcontroller interfacing and programming; design and development of motion control for mechatronics systems including PLC; introduction of integrated complex mechatronics systems: concept, structure, and applications. Through a design project, students will use and apply these concepts in building a complex mechatronics system with advanced features.
ENGG*4480.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4490.title: Sampled Data Control Design
ENGG*4490.description: This course introduces the theory and techniques required to analyze, design, and implement sampled data controllers on real, continuous time systems. Topics include sampling, system identification and modeling, delay, state-space and frequency domain approaches to control, emulation methods, and direct z-domain methods. In the course project, students will apply the techniques discussed in class to design and implement a computer based controller for a real physical system, then compare the actual results obtained to the expected theoretical results and discuss sources of error and limitations of their approach.
ENGG*4490.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the ESC specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4510.title: Assessment and Management of Risk
ENGG*4510.description: This course will develop the bases by which risk to human health and the environment can be assessed. Issues of hazardous waste cleanups, permitting of water and air discharges, food safety, flood protection, as examples, are addressed. The course also examines how decisions are made to manage the risks to acceptable levels.
ENGG*4510.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4510.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4540.title: Advanced Computer Architecture
ENGG*4540.description: This course covers topics such as: basics of pipeline structure, advanced pipelining and instruction level parallelism, multiprocessor and thread-level parallelism, memory-hierarchy design (main memory, virtual memory, caches), storage systems, interconnection networks, multiprocessor architectures (centralized and distributed). Advanced topics related to new emerging computer architectures will also be presented. The emphasis in each topic is on fundamental limitations and the trade-offs involved in designing computer systems, including memory and processing bandwidth, network bandwidth and latency, synchronization, and storage system bandwidth and latency.
ENGG*4540.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4540.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4550.title: VLSI Digital Design
ENGG*4550.description: This course introduces the students to the analysis, synthesis and design of Very Large Scale integration (VLSI) digital circuits and implementing them in silicon. The topics of this course are presented at three levels of design abstraction. At device level: MOS diode; MOS (FET) transistor; interconnect wire. At circuit level: CMOS inverter; static CMOS gates (NAND, NOR); dynamic gates (NAND, NOR); static latches and registers; dynamic latches and registers; pipelining principles and circuit styles; BICMOS logic circuits. At system level; implementation strategies for digital ICs; interconnect at system level; timing issues in digital circuits (clock structures); the adder; the multiplier; the shifter; memory design and array structure; low power design circuits and architectures.
ENGG*4550.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the CENG specialization in the BENG and BENG:C programs. See department for more information. Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4550.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4560.title: Embedded System Design
ENGG*4560.description: This course introduces the basic principles of embedded system design. It utilizes advanced hardware/software abstractions to help design complex systems. Topics include: design of embedded CUPs; embedded architecture cores; system-on-chip designs and integration using processor cores and dedicated core modules; embedded computing platforms; embedded programming design and analysis; processes and operating systems; networks for embedded systems; distributed embedded architectures; design examples that target robotics, automobile, and communication systems.
ENGG*4560.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4560.department: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ENGG*4580.title: Sustainable Energy Systems Design
ENGG*4580.description: The analysis and design of sustainable energy systems are presented in this course. Techniques considered include generation of alternative designs to satisfy a problem definition; evaluation of alternative designs; application of modeling simulations and cost analyses.
ENGG*4580.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4590.title: Sensors Instrumentation and Measurements
ENGG*4590.description: This course explores the fundamentals of sensing and instrumentation technology, including measurements, mechanisms, and principles of instrumentation with applications to electrical and mechanical systems. The course will discuss transducers, signal conditioning, data acquisition systems and will include examples of measurement of physical quantities such as temperature, force, torque, position, speed, acceleration, pressure, humidity, flow and liquid level.
ENGG*4590.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs.

ENGG*4630.title: Asset Management and Infrastructure Rehabilitation
