ENGG*4630.description: This course covers strategies used to protect and extend the service life of long-term public infrastructure assets, like transportation networks and water and waste systems, that are essential to quality of life and the economy. It addresses adaptation approaches to increase the resilience of infrastructure to climate change. Students are introduced to innovative technologies used to monitor infrastructure performance and models used to predict deterioration and quality of service. Infrastructure asset management emphasizes the later stages of infrastructure's life cycle and includes maintenance, rehabilitation and replacement. Students develop proficiency with software tools used to support infrastructure asset management and decision-making.
ENGG*4630.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits
ENGG*4630.department: Department of Civil Environmental and Water Resources Eng

ENGG*4660.title: Medical Image Processing
ENGG*4660.description: This course covers the fundamentals of medical image processing. Image processing topics covered include: fundamentals of resolution and quantization; linear systems as applied to multi-dimensional continuous and discrete systems; point operations such as contrast enhancement and histogram equalization; geometric operations for distortion correction, including interpolation methods; linear filtering in both the spatial and spatial-frequency domains; and image restoration and inverse filtering. Image segmentation is covered in the framework of pattern recognition using single and multiple dimensional features, and includes the fundamental Bayes classifier as well as machine learning methods for both supervised and unsupervised learning.
ENGG*4660.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4670.title: Geotechnical and Geoenvironmental Design
ENGG*4670.description: This course applies geotechnical and hydrogeological principles to the design and analysis of footings, piles, and foundations for buildings, retaining walls, roads and solid and liquid waste disposal facilities. Software tools will be used to support design and analysis.
ENGG*4670.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGG*4680.title: Multidisciplinary Engineering Design
ENGG*4680.description: This is a general design course for students registered in the B. Eng. major in Biomedical Engineering and who do not wish to develop a strong specialization in one of the specific areas of the program. Students work in groups to develop a general Biomedical engineering design. Special attention is paid to the sustainability of the design, its economic feasibility and overall efficiency.
ENGG*4680.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4690.title: Mechatronic Systems Design II
ENGG*4690.description: This course covers advanced topics in the design of mechatronics systems. The course is based on a case studies approach. These include design of microelectromechanical accelerometers, micro positioning systems, and smart material based electrical actuators.
ENGG*4690.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4740.title: Computational Methods for Data Analysis
ENGG*4740.description: This survey course introduces proven, stable computational methods for data analysis of large datasets in engineering. Algorithms to wrangle and analyze engineering data (time history, field variables), images, and motion will be introduced. Algorithms will be implemented in a computing environment and a case study in engineering data analysis will be carried out.
ENGG*4740.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to students in the BENG.MTE and BENG.MTE:C programs. Non- BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4750.title: Power Electronics
ENGG*4750.description: This course focuses on power electronics and solid-state converters, covering key topics such as switching devices, including Silicon Controlled Rectifiers, Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors, Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistors, and Gate Turn-ff Thyristors. It explores non-isolated and isolated DC-DC switched-mode converters, diode and thyristor bridges, as well as voltage-sourced converters like inverters and rectifiers. The curriculum emphasizes the practical applications of power electronics in industry, alongside discussions on typical digital and analog control schemes for power electronic converters. To reinforce understanding, important concepts are demonstrated through computer simulations and hands-on hardware labs.

ENGG*4760.title: Biological Wastewater Treatment Design
ENGG*4760.description: The course applies design principles for a variety of biological treatment systems for both municipal and industrial wastewater. This involves the design of suspended growth and attached growth processes, anaerobic digestion, sludge processing and utilization, water reuse and resource recovery facilities.
ENGG*4760.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4760.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4770.title: Physical and Chemical Water and Wastewater Treatment Design
ENGG*4770.description: This course focuses on the theory, application, and design principles of physical and chemical operations and processes for the treatment of water and wastewater. This involves the design of physical and chemical unit operations, and evaluating the optimum combination to satisfy the given design constraints and criteria. The optimum designs integrate engineering science, basic science, economics, and health and safety for workers and the public.
ENGG*4770.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4770.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4780.title: Smart Integrated System Design
ENGG*4780.description: This course introduces the principles and techniques used in the design, simulation, fabrication and testing of integrated circuits (IC) for smart electronic systems. Design flows and computer-aided design tools will be considered with practical applications to the design of building cells, filters, data converters and array subsystems. Topics covered include noise and linearity analysis in integrated circuits, stability and frequency compensation, mixed-signal circuit design, amplifier and filter design, and Nyquist-rate and oversampling data converters. Students will gain hands-on experience through a design project where they will simulate, layout, and test an IC. Emphasis is placed on understanding performance metrics such as gain, bandwidth, power consumption, and noise, as well as on the practical constraints involved in IC fabrication and testing. Students will develop a solid foundation in both the theoretical and practical aspects of integrated circuit design, preparing them for careers in microelectronics, smart device development and system integration.

ENGG*4790.title: Control Systems and Automation Design
ENGG*4790.description: This course explores the fundamental concepts and practical applications of control systems and automation design. Students will learn how to design and optimize control systems for various industries, focusing on feedback control, system dynamics, and automation technologies. Through hands-on projects and real-world case studies, participants will gain the skills necessary to develop efficient, stable, and reliable automated systems, preparing them for careers in industrial automation, robotics, and system integration.

ENGG*4800.title: Electrical Energy Systems Design
ENGG*4800.description: This course delves into the physical and design principles of energy- related products and systems, such as lighting, mobile phones, and photovoltaic systems. Students will participate in interactive, hands- on activities that include reverse engineering, redesigning existing products, analyzing data, and utilizing modeling and simulation tools. Through a multidisciplinary approach to sustainable energy design, the course emphasizes the development of innovative solutions to real-world challenges, making it ideal for students who wish to blend technical expertise with creative problem-solving skills.

ENGG*4810.title: Control of Atmospheric Particulates
ENGG*4810.description: The focus of this course is understanding, analyzing and designing conventional and innovative atmospheric particulate control systems. The properties and transport of atmospheric particulates, and the principles of cyclones, filtration and electrostatic precipitation will be taught through theory, simulations, experiments and a design project.

prerequisites.raw: 6.00 credits of ENGG courses, ENGG*2230, MATH*2130, (ENGG*2450 or PHYS*1010)

ENGG*4810.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4810.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4820.title: Atmospheric Emission Control: Combustion Systems
ENGG*4820.description: Combustion systems are an essential part of our society, however, they are also the dominant source of atmospheric pollutants. This course will focus on investigation of combustion systems for the purpose of reducing atmospheric emissions.
ENGG*4820.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.
ENGG*4820.department: Department of Civil, Environmental, and Water Resources Engineering

ENGG*4830.title: Modelling Complex Systems for Civil Engineering
ENGG*4830.description: This course explores the application of systems thinking to civil infrastructure challenges. Key topics include systems theory, complex adaptive systems, systems tools, and systems approaches. The course will emphasize the role of computational modelling and simulation as a central tool for applying systems thinking to civil engineering challenges. Students will learn techniques for modeling structural frameworks and transportation and water resources networks, including dynamic interactions and time-dependent behaviours. Students will become familiar with tools to optimize performance and inform decision-making. The importance of quantifying uncertainty and managing risks will be emphasized.
ENGG*4830.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGG*4840.title: Sensor Networks and Internet of Things for Civil Engineering
ENGG*4840.description: This course examines the application of smart sensors and emerging technologies in civil engineering, focusing on how these advanced tools can enhance the monitoring, management, and performance of infrastructure systems. Students will gain an understanding of various types of sensors, such as strain gauges, accelerometers, and environmental sensors, and their applications in civil engineering. Students will explore IoT technologies, network protocols, data acquisition systems, and sensor deployment strategies tailored to monitor structural health and infrastructure conditions. Case studies will examine the implementation of intelligent systems in transportation, energy, utilities, and Smart Cities to improve efficiency, reduce environmental impacts, and enhance social equity.
ENGG*4840.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGG*4850.title: Humanitarian Engineering
ENGG*4850.description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of humanitarian engineering. It examines the conditions associated with different types of humanitarian emergencies and disasters in various socio- political contexts. Inquiry-based projects allow for the exploration of alternative approaches to address the engineering challenges associated with humanitarian emergencies and disasters. Emphasis is placed on improving the safety and well-being of marginalized communities.
ENGG*4850.restriction: Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGG*4870.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Internet of Things in Automation
ENGG*4870.description: This course introduces the principles and applications of artificial intelligence (AI) and information technology (IT) in modern automation systems. Topics include machine learning, robotic process automation (RPA), cloud-based automation platforms, and Internet of Things (IoT) integration. Through a combination of theoretical foundations and practical, hands-on activities, students develop the ability to design, implement, and evaluate intelligent automation solutions for industrial and cyber-physical systems. The course also examines ethical, legal, and societal considerations associated with the deployment of AI-enabled automation technologies.

ENGG*4911.title: Civil Engineering and Design IV
ENGG*4911.description: This is the first half of the capstone design course for the Civil Engineering program. Competencies in professionalism and ethics and equity will be advanced through the examination of diverse Civil Engineering case studies. The course will further explore tools and approaches for the planning, construction, and management of Civil Engineering projects and infrastructure. It will examine tendering processes and contract management. Students will also prepare a proposal for the design project that will be completed in the winter term. Students will form teams, normally of four members, with other students in civil or any other engineering major. The proposal must define the design problem, including safety, environmental, social, and economic dimensions, and set out a detailed work plan. Design teams are responsible for securing a faculty advisor, who has a P.Eng..

prerequisites.raw: All 1000 and 2000 level BENG.CIVE core courses, ENGG*3100

ENGG*4911.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, School of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4912.title: Civil Engineering and Design IV
ENGG*4912.description: This is the second half of the capstone design course for the Civil Engineering program. Ethics and legal case studies relevant to professional engineering practice are presented during the lectures. Design teams will continue through the design process to prepare a completely specified solution, at the level of preliminary or final design, to the problem defined in the first half of the course. Through a final report and presentation, design teams provide an evidence-based defense of their design, including its primary function and performance related to safety and sustainability (economic, environmental, and social).
ENGG*4912.restriction: Registration in the BENG program. Students must have a minimum cumulative average of 60% or higher in ALL ENGG courses. Restriction waiver requests are handled by the Director, School of Engineering, or designate.

ENGG*4950.title: Remote Sensing and Spatial Data Analysis
ENGG*4950.description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of spatial analysis and remote sensing techniques. Students will develop skills in processing and interpreting spatial data. The course also covers the application of AI techniques to spatial data analysis and the use of drone technology for image capture and analysis. Practical applications are emphasized, including urban planning (e.g., land use/infrastructure development), disaster management (e.g., flood mapping and emergency response), agriculture (e.g., precision farming), and civil engineering (e.g., infrastructure monitoring). Industry-standard GIS and remote sensing software tools will be utilized.
ENGG*4950.restriction: ENGG*3340; Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGG*4960.title: Water Infrastructure Design for Cities
ENGG*4960.description: This course applies the principles of hydrology and hydraulics to urban water systems. Students will estimate water demands and wastewater flows. They will use computer simulation tools to evaluate water pressure and quality within water distribution networks under a range of operating conditions. Students will use models to estimate urban stormwater flows and aid design of sewers and stormwater management facilities. The course addresses the deterioration and rehabilitation of water infrastructure. It considers the implications of climate change for wet weather flows and explores the role of urban water infrastructure in achieving climate resilient and socially just communities.
ENGG*4960.restriction: ENGG*4370; Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits.

ENGG*4970.title: Green Infrastructure Design for Stormwater Management
ENGG*4970.description: In this course, students apply an understanding of the effects of urbanization on the hydrologic cycle and the importance of different sized storms to design and demonstrate the effectiveness of stormwater management systems. Green infrastructure design includes aspects of urban planning to reduce impacts on the quality and quantity of stormwater runoff. Students learn to select types of engineered facilities and their materials and design features for their ability to achieve water quantity and quality objectives, climate resilience and other environmental and social co-benefits. Students learn to size facilities like green roofs, infiltration trenches, permeable pavements, bioretention cells, vegetated swales, as well as stormwater ponds, and represent them in models. Continuous and event-based modelling are used to aid design and demonstrate the overall effectiveness of systems to prevent increased risks of flooding and erosion and protect water quality and aquatic ecosystems.
ENGG*4970.restriction: ENGG*4370; Non-BENG students may take a maximum of 4.00 ENGG credits

ENGL*1030.title: Effective Writing
ENGL*1030.description: This course is designed primarily for those not pursuing a specialization in English and focuses on the development of the skills for effective writing. Topics will include sentence and paragraph structure, essay organization, and the implementation of convincing written arguments. Students will apply these skills to the completion of several written assignments, culminating in a short work of non-fiction prose.
ENGL*1030.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*1080.title: Foundations in Critical Reading and Writing
ENGL*1080.description: The creative use of language in literature, cinema, and related media enables us to explore the issues that have mobilized communities in different eras and English-speaking regions of the globe. Using case studies on issues that resonate from the past into the present, students will employ close reading methods to investigate how language gives form to ideas, and use academic writing as a vehicle for conveying an argument about these ideas. Reading- and writing-intensive course.
ENGL*1080.restriction: This is a priority access course. Enrolment in the fall semester may be restricted to students registered in the English major, minor, area of concentration, Creative Writing minor, or in semesters one or two of the BA or BAS program.
ENGL*1080.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*1200.title: Reading the Contemporary World
ENGL*1200.description: This course, which is designed primarily for those not planning a specialization in English, introduces students to literary texts and persuasive forms of writing, bringing to the fore some of the links between language and contemporary social and political issues. Course materials will represent diversity in terms of national origins, gender, race, and class. The course emphasizes the use of figurative language as well as the development of students' critical reading and writing skills. Students planning to major or minor or pursue an area of concentration in English must take ENGL*1080 and ENGL*2080, but may also take ENGL*1200 and count it as an elective lecture.
ENGL*1200.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*1500.title: Medicine and Literature
ENGL*1500.description: This course explores the representational and cultural practices concerning health care and the body. Students will examine how literary texts represent, interpret, and critique the cultures of biomedical science, the clinic, and the hospital. Areas of interest include disease, illness, health, disability, and psychological and physiological trauma.
ENGL*1500.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2040.title: Latinx Literature: Myth, Magic and Memory
ENGL*2040.description: This course introduces students to English-language literature produced by writers of Latin American descent in both the United States and Canada. Students will focus on Latinx writers who endeavor to maintain ethnic traditions and ties to Latin America by engaging myth, magic, and memory in their fiction. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*2040.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2080.title: Foundations in Literary Scholarship
ENGL*2080.description: This course revisits the historical, national, and genre sweep of ENGL*1080 by conducting a range of specific, more in-depth studies of particular works (including some of the texts examined in ENGL*1080) in their historical moments. Seminars help students to develop a range of critical approaches through oral presentations and essay-writing.
ENGL*2080.restriction: Restricted to students in the ENGL major, minor, area of concentration
ENGL*2080.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2090.title: Studies in Shakespeare
ENGL*2090.description: This course focuses on Shakespeare as both a writer and an enduring cultural presence. It may, at the instructor's choice, focus on works by Shakespeare as well as works by others in response to Shakespeare, in Shakespeare's original historical context or in more contemporary contexts. The course may address, among others, genre, performance, adaptation, the politics of Shakespearean interpretation, including issues such as gender, subjectivity, empire, and nation. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*2090.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2120.title: Seminar: Critical Practices
ENGL*2120.description: This course guides students through a range of critical approaches and explores their implications for readings of a limited number of literary texts. The seminar's main areas of concentration are: (1) close reading, centering on the way a particular poem, work of fiction, or play works in its details and overall structure; (2) critical approaches and methodologies; (3) critical writing and discussion. (Choices of approaches and texts will be determined by individual instructors.) Writing- and presentation-intensive course.
ENGL*2120.restriction: Registration in the English major, minor or area of concentration.
ENGL*2120.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2130.title: Seminar: Literature and Social Change
ENGL*2130.description: This course explores the social and cultural work that literary texts perform. Seminars will illuminate such categories as gender, sexuality, nation, race, ethnicity, and class; particular ways in which they are written into a limited number of literary works; and some of the critical debates surrounding our interpretations of those processes. (Choices of approaches and texts will be determined by individual instructors.) Writing- and presentation-intensive course.
ENGL*2130.restriction: Registration in the English major, minor or area of concentration.
ENGL*2130.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2190.title: Queer Literatures and Cultures
ENGL*2190.description: This course examines the ways in which gender, sexuality, and identity politics are addressed, critiqued, and shaped by queer literature, performance and media. This course will consider the meanings of "queer" itself and explore how queerness intersects with race, class, gender, sex, and other forms of social difference.
ENGL*2190.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2200.title: Postcolonial Literatures, Film, and Other Media
ENGL*2200.description: This course introduces students to contemporary literatures, film, and other media that engage with the impact of European colonization on eighty-five percent of the globe. The course may include material from Africa, Australia, the Americas, the Caribbean, the Indian Subcontinent, and the Pacific. Reading-intensive course
ENGL*2200.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2260.title: Law and Literature
ENGL*2260.description: This course introduces students to the intersections between law and literature. The course encourages students to read literary texts by focusing on issues of justice, crime, judgment, and equity. Students will examine how literature can serve as an alternative cultural arena or site that gives voice to experiences and knowledges that cannot be translated into the law's language of equivalence and neutrality. By reading literary texts in relation to the law, students will examine how literature and literary theory can enhance our understanding of the law.
ENGL*2260.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2280.title: Sporting Bodies
ENGL*2280.description: This course examines various aspects of sports and play as they are manifested in a range of literary texts and other cultural forms. Because the emergence of sport as a social practice has been so intertwined with notions of regulation, performance, and fandom, sporting culture offers a valuable site for looking at questions of identity and social relations more broadly.
ENGL*2280.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2310.title: Literature and the Supernatural
ENGL*2310.description: While we often dismiss the occult or the supernatural in literature as simple entertainment, this course encourages students to examine how literary works engage the occult and the supernatural in order to address issues of power, race, gender, imperialism and modernity from the nineteenth century to the present.
ENGL*2310.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2360.title: The Medieval World
ENGL*2360.description: This course introduces students to literature before 1500 by considering how medieval writers imagined and moved across the world. Students will read poetry, drama, travel writing, and scientific texts written in and beyond England. The course will situate these texts in their historical contexts, approaching premodern societies as dynamic, diverse, and interconnected. Topics will vary but may include intercultural contact and colonization, gender and sexuality, technology, medicine, and views of nature.
ENGL*2360.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2380.title: Reading Poetry
ENGL*2380.description: This course offers an introduction to the challenges posed by poetic discourse and provides students with the practical tools they need to analyze and appreciate verse. Students will read and analyze a broad range of verse practice in English, thereby gaining a base repertoire through which they can approach future encounters with poetry in other classes. Significant portions of the course will be devoted to thinking about poetry in historical terms.
ENGL*2380.restriction: Registration in the English major, minor or area of concentration, or Creative Writing major or minor.
ENGL*2380.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2550.title: Indigenous Literatures of North America
ENGL*2550.description: This course explores selected issues, perspectives, and voices within the study of Indigenous literatures and their contexts in North America. One purpose of the course is to raise questions about the meaning of the U.S. - Canadian border for Indigenous writers. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*2550.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2640.title: Culture, Location, Identity: Minoritized Literatures in Canada and Beyond
ENGL*2640.description: This course will open up debates around emergent issues, perspectives, and voices in the literatures of minoritized cultures particularly within the North American context. Questions about the meanings of various borders for understanding Canadian negotiations of identity, culture, and location will remain a consistent feature of this variable content course. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*2640.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2740.title: Children's Literature
ENGL*2740.description: This course serves as an introduction to the critical study of children's literature and culture. Focusing on selected genres of, issues in, or theoretical approaches to literature for children, this variable-content course explores shifting (and often conflicting) conceptions of childhood, in general, and of children as readers and cultural consumers. Reading- intensive course.
ENGL*2740.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*2880.title: Women in Literature
ENGL*2880.description: This variable-content course will involve the study and discussion of poems, stories, novels and plays by or about women. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*2880.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3000.title: Editorial Experience
ENGL*3000.description: This experiential-learning course will provide a combination of theoretical background and practical, hands-on experience in the field of literary magazine editing and publishing. Students will produce an undergraduate literary journal devoted to showcasing the writing of the University of Guelph community. Duties involved in the production of the journal include soliciting and evaluating submissions, editing, proofreading, publicity, and more. Students will study the art and technique of editing through reading essays by editors, editor-author correspondence, and original and edited texts, as well as gaining an understanding of the current magazine landscape.
ENGL*3000.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3100.title: Reading 2.0: Electronic Lit, Games, and Digital Narrative Forms
ENGL*3100.description: This course introduces the methods and debates concerning digital forms of writing, and digital methods of literary analysis and knowledge creation. Studies will encounter and analyze one or more digital forms of writing such as digital poetry, e-literature, hypertext fiction, digital recordings, video games, or podcasts.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030).

ENGL*3100.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3200.title: The Fairy Tale
ENGL*3200.description: This course introduces students to the multiple, varied, and unstable understandings of the "fairy tale," focusing on the history of the term in the English language; the various ways in which the genre's form, function, and meaning have been defined and debated over the past three centuries; and the range of forms (oral, written, theatrical, cinematic, etc.) that have been considered part of the genre's intertextual web.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3200.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3240.title: Studies in Early Modern Literature and Culture
ENGL*3240.description: This course examines various aspects of the literature and culture of early modern England. The course may examine, at the instructor's choice, some part of the fifteenth, sixteenth and seventeenth centuries and may focus on a variety of issues, including gender and sexuality, poetics and politics, religion and authority, nation and empire, and their relation to literary production. The genres and writers examined will vary.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3240.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3270.title: Writing Britain 1660-1914
ENGL*3270.description: This course addresses British literature in relation to cultural debates and concerns of the long eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. Working within a subset of this period, the course will address topics such as politics and literature, revolution and resistance, satire, gender and sexuality, institutional power, domesticity and the evolution of literary forms or movements. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3270.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3320.title: Romanticism to Victorianism: Culture and Conformity
ENGL*3320.description: This course explores the key texts in various genres of British cultural debates of the late eighteenth to the late nineteenth century. Focal points may include: literary representations of family and society; science and narrative; Britain's "others"; class and conflict; protest and power; the roots of modernism; European influences. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3320.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3340.title: British Imperial Culture
ENGL*3340.description: This multi-genre course introduces students to the literature of British imperialism in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The course will consider the changing relationship between nation, empire, and colony by examining literary representations of such topics as: orientalism; travel writing; the construction of race; the representation of trade; the popular literature of empire; children's literature; the question of the other. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3340.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3380.title: Studies in the History of Literary Production
ENGL*3380.description: This course will examine a range of issues arising from the materiality of book production and circulation. Topics may include serialization; mass production and circulation; patronage; reviewing; circulating libraries; licensing; censorship; children's literature; periodicals; gift books; letters; and other aspects of publishing and the public sphere. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3380.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3390.title: Contemporary Literature and the Environment
ENGL*3390.description: This course explores literary depictions of the natural environment. Topics may include climate change, fossil fuels, human/animal relations, meteorological disasters, extinction, agriculture, and scientific ethics. Students will have opportunities to put theory into practice through critical writing and public outreach. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3390.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3420.title: 20th- & 21st-Century Drama
ENGL*3420.description: This course offers a selective study of 20th- and/or 21st-century play- scripts written in English. Students are advised to consult the web- descriptions for the particular focus of the course's offering.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including [0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030) or 0.50 credits in Theatre Studies]

ENGL*3420.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3470.title: 20th- and 21st Century British Literature
ENGL*3470.description: This multigenre course explores aesthetic and socio-cultural movements in British literature from the twentieth century to the present. Reading- intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3470.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3540.title: Writing the United States
ENGL*3540.description: This multigenre course explores the relationship between literary production and political power from the emergence of U.S. culture through the long nineteenth century. Areas of focus may include national fantasy; the literatures of war, imperial expansion, captivity, and genocide; narratives of race and immigration; the cult of domesticity; and the rise of mass culture. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3540.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3550.title: Modern United States Literatures
ENGL*3550.description: This multigenre course explores powerful examples of modern United States literatures, from about the First World War to the present. The selection is wide, including both traditional and experimental forms; female and male writers from various ethnic and racial groups; and a range of cultural issues. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3550.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3570.title: Chaucer in Context
ENGL*3570.description: This course will introduce students to significant aspects of Chaucer's writing read in the context of works by Chaucer's precursors and near contemporaries. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3570.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3600.title: Historical Literature and the Environment
ENGL*3600.description: Using literature and non-fiction prose written primarily before 1900, this course addresses the relationship between print culture and the environment. Readings are not confined to a specific national literature and may be chosen to address transnational thought or the non-national spaces of the ocean and polar regions. Topics will vary but may address industrialization, human/animal relationships, resource extraction, plantation ecologies, specimen collection, extinction, scientific thought, or encounter with unfamiliar environments.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3600.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3630.title: Writing Canada: Forging the Nation
ENGL*3630.description: This multigenre course focuses on Canadian literature to World War One, examining cultural contestation in the Canadian settler colony among the Canadian, U.S., British, and indigenous peoples. Topics may include the rise of nationalist discourse, race and nation, landscape and the sublime, gothic, sentimental, and historical fiction, children's literature, slavery and resistance narratives, travel and captivity narratives. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3630.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3680.title: 20th- & 21st-Century Canadian Literature and Criticism
ENGL*3680.description: This course examines Canadian literature and criticism in English from the beginnings of the twentieth century to the present in relation to a variety of social, cultural, and historical contexts. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3680.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3750.title: Studies in Postcolonial Literatures
ENGL*3750.description: This course is a concentrated study in a major sub-area of postcolonial literature. Specific topics will vary each year, but may involve focus on a particular genre or region such as Africa, Australia, Canada, the Caribbean, India, and the Pacific. Reading-intensive course.

prerequisites.raw: 7.50 credits including 0.50 credits in English (excluding ENGL*1030)

ENGL*3750.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3870.title: Topics in Literary and Cultural Studies
ENGL*3870.description: These variable-content courses provide opportunities for topics in cross- period studies, inter- and transdisciplinary studies and cultural studies, among others that are not available in regular offerings. Consult the School for specific offerings in a given semester. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*3870.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3880.title: Topics in Literary and Cultural Studies
ENGL*3880.description: These variable-content courses provide opportunities for topics in cross- period studies, inter- and transdisciplinary studies and cultural studies, among others that are not available in regular offerings. Consult the School for specific offerings in a given semester. Reading-intensive course.
ENGL*3880.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3940.title: Seminar: Genre in Cultural Context
ENGL*3940.description: This variable-content seminar focuses on conventions of literatures, film or media as they inform our interactions with a text. Seminars will explore the impact of generic conventions on the processes of production, reception, and interpretation of meaning. Activities involve historically and critically informed close readings of a limited number of texts. (Choice of focus and texts to be determined by individual instructors.) Writing- and presentation-intensive course.
ENGL*3940.restriction: Registration in the English major, minor or area of concentration.
ENGL*3940.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*3960.title: Seminar: Literature in History
ENGL*3960.description: This variable-content course explores the processes by which specific texts or genres are imbedded in their time. Seminars may focus on such topics as the archive surrounding one text, memory writing, or emergent genres in order to negotiate theoretical problems pertaining to historicization, rhetorical reading, narratology, gender/sexuality studies or the racialization of national fantasy. (Choice of period and texts will be determined by individual instructors.) Writing- and presentation-intensive course.
ENGL*3960.restriction: Registration in the English major, minor or area of concentration.
ENGL*3960.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4090.title: Digital Literary Studies
ENGL*4090.description: A seminar designed to allow students to delve into a particular aspect of digital literary studies in depth, understanding the critical debate surrounding it, experimenting with techniques or tools associated with it, and reflecting critically on its implications. Topics will vary and may include: Writing in the Digital Age; Topic Modelling for Literary Studies; Literary Networks; and Digital Humanities, Feminisms and Diversity. The course will engage aspects of digital textuality and literary study such as: e-literature; interactive digital narrative, including video games; non- linear long-form arguments, including hypertext; semantic text encoding; exploration of several digital writing platforms; the rhetoric of interface and the materiality of digital media; text analysis; and visualization.
ENGL*4090.restriction: Restricted to students in the English or Culture and Technology Studies major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in ENGL and CTS. English majors can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in ENGL at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4090.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4240.title: Medieval and Early Modern Literatures
ENGL*4240.description: This seminar provides the opportunity for intensive study of British literature from the beginnings to 1660. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4240.restriction: ENGL*4040. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4240.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4250.title: 18th- and 19th- Century Literatures
ENGL*4250.description: This seminar provides the opportunity for intensive study of British literature from 1660 to 1900. Students are advised to complete a 3000- level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4250.restriction: ENGL*4050. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4250.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4270.title: United States Literatures
ENGL*4270.description: This seminar provides the opportunity for intensive study of United States literatures. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4270.restriction: ENGL*4070. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4270.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4280.title: Canadian Literatures
ENGL*4280.description: This seminar provides the opportunity for intensive study of Canadian literatures. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4280.restriction: ENGL*4080. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4280.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4310.title: Special Studies in English
ENGL*4310.description: A seminar designed to provide students in semesters 7 and 8 with an opportunity to pursue studies in an area or areas of language or literature not available in other courses. The course may be taught by a visiting professor or members of the school.
ENGL*4310.restriction: ENGL*4100, ENGL*4110. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4310.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4400.title: Postcolonial Literatures
ENGL*4400.description: This course provides the opportunity for intensive study of a representative selection of literature in English by writers from Africa, India, the Caribbean, Australia, and the Pacific. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4400.restriction: ENGL*4200. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4400.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4410.title: Modern & Contemporary Literatures
ENGL*4410.description: This course provides the opportunity for a study of significant works in fiction, poetry, and drama that demonstrate new approaches in form and content characteristic of 20th- and 21st-century writings in English. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4410.restriction: ENGL*4210. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4410.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4420.title: Women's Writings
ENGL*4420.description: This course provides for intensive study of issues relating to the aesthetic strategies, such as those associated with structure, imagery, and language, devised by women writers to reflect women's experience and perceptions. Students are advised to complete a 3000-level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4420.restriction: ENGL*4220. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4420.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4500.title: Non-fiction Prose
ENGL*4500.description: This course offers an intensive study of non-fiction prose. Topics to be explored may include the roles and contexts of public and/or private writing, the role of literary criticism in reading texts sometimes marked as non-literary, the history of non-fictional prose forms, or the formal or ideological uses of the distinctions between fact and fiction.
ENGL*4500.restriction: ENGL*4300. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4500.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4810.title: Directed Reading
ENGL*4810.description: This course is intended particularly as preparation for ENGL*4910. The student will design a course of readings and assignments with the instructor, whose consent must first be obtained. This option is intended only for students who have performed particularly well within the honours program. Exceptional students may take ENGL*4810 in preparation for a ENGL*4910 creative writing project, on the approval of the instructor.
ENGL*4810.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4880.title: 20th- & 21st-Century Poetry
ENGL*4880.description: This seminar provides opportunities to study English-language modern and contemporary poetry. Students are advised to complete a 3000- level lecture course in this subject area prior to enrolling in the 4000-level course.
ENGL*4880.restriction: ENGL*4680. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4880.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4890.title: Contemporary Literary Theory
ENGL*4890.description: This course will study the major branches of contemporary literary theory. Topics covered will include structuralism, reader-oriented theory, feminist theory, new historicist and materialist critique, postcolonialist critique, and deconstruction.
ENGL*4890.restriction: ENGL*4690. Restricted to students in the English major, who have completed 14.00 credits with an average of 70% in all course attempts in English. Students can register in a maximum of 2.00 credits in English at the 4000 level.
ENGL*4890.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENGL*4910.title: Honours English Essay
ENGL*4910.description: A major essay (approx. 25 pages) on some subject of special interest to the student is prepared and written under the direction of a faculty member. Consent of the instructor must be obtained and the subject must be approved by the School prior to the semester in which the course is to be taken. This option is intended only for students who have performed particularly well within the honours program. Exceptional students may use ENGL*4910 for creative writing, on the approval of the instructor.
ENGL*4910.department: SSchool of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

ENVM*4500.title: Environmental Management Integrated Project
ENVM*4500.description: Students will apply the skills and knowledge gained in earlier courses to analyzing environmental processes and issues associated with an industrial, municipal, or agricultural enterprise. Students will work in teams to complete a detailed environmental report with policy recommendations and action plans.

ENVS*1030.title: Introduction to Environmental Sciences
ENVS*1030.description: This course introduces students to what it means to think critically from an environmental sciences perspective. It covers an introduction to: philosophy of science, philosophy of the environment, the evaluation of scientific evidence, and scientific arguments. The course focuses on how we understand and recognize environmental problems, how we decide what to do about them, and what role science can and should play in these discussions.
ENVS*1030.restriction: ENVS*1100. Registration in the BSC(Env) program or BBRM.EM.

ENVS*1040.title: Natural History of the Great Lakes Region
ENVS*1040.description: This course is an introduction to the ecological regions, habitats and biodiversity of the Great Lakes watershed with a focus on Southern and Central Ontario. Beginning with a survey of the physical environment of the region, including topography, geomorphology and glaciation, soils, hydrology and climate, the course explores each of the major ecological regions and habitats of the watershed in terms of their defining characteristics and biological communities, including representative flora and fauna.
ENVS*1040.restriction: Restricted to students in BIESP, BIESP:C.

ENVS*1060.title: Discovering Planet Earth
ENVS*1060.description: This course provides an introduction to geological principles, their historical development and application to interpreting Earth materials and processes. This course is suitable for those wishing a general knowledge of Earth sciences.
ENVS*1060.restriction: Not available to students registered in the BBRM, BIESP, BSC or BSC(Env) programs.

ENVS*1100.title: Fundamentals of Environmental Sciences
ENVS*1100.description: This course introduces students to what it means to think critically from an environmental sciences perspective. It covers an introduction to: philosophy of science, philosophy of the environment, the evaluation of scientific evidence, and scientific arguments. The course focuses on how we understand and recognize environmental problems, how we decide what to do about them, and what role science can and should play in these discussions.
ENVS*1100.restriction: ENVS*1030.Not available to students registered in the B.Sc.(Env.), BIESP, and BBRM.EM programs.

ENVS*2030.title: Meteorology and Climatology
ENVS*2030.description: This course examines solar and terrestrial radiation; pressure systems and winds; atmospheric stability and vertical motions; air masses and fronts; clouds and precipitation; selected topics in applied meteorology including air pollution. The laboratory emphasizes the analysis and use of atmospheric data for solving environmental problems.

prerequisites.raw: 1 of IPS*1500, MATH*1080, MATH*1200, PHYS*1070, PHYS*1080, PHYS*1130, PHYS*1300

ENVS*2040.title: Plant Health and the Environment
ENVS*2040.description: This is an interdisciplinary course on the nature and importance of diseases, insects and abiotic stresses on plant productivity and quality. A case history approach will be used to illustrate the biology of plant pests, the principles of pest population management, and related topics.

prerequisites.raw: BIOL*1040 or (2 of BIOL*1050, BIOL*1070, BIOL*1080, BIOL*1090)

ENVS*2060.title: Soil Science
ENVS*2060.description: This course is an introduction to the principles of soil science - the origin of soils, their classification and interpretation in natural and modified environments. Soil will be studied as a product of the natural environment, with a focus on formation processes and changes which occur when it is modified through use. A variety of uses including agriculture, forestry, recreation, and urban development will be considered.

ENVS*2070.title: Environmental Ethics and Perspectives
ENVS*2070.description: This course provides an interdisciplinary approach to environmental issues that focus on normative questions of human perspective and choice. Students will investigate the ethical issues that arise from the impact of our actions on the natural world. The course will analyze and assess various influential approaches to developing a normative understanding, including those of philosophers, economists, indigenous communities, feminists, artists, and scientists. Students will develop the necessary intellectual skills to be able to understand and engage fruitfully in normative debates about the environment.

ENVS*2080.title: Introduction to Environmental Microbiology
ENVS*2080.description: This course will introduce students to environmental microbiology, with a focus on the important roles of microorganisms in various environments such as soil, water and sediments. Discussion will emphasize the physiology, biochemistry, molecular biology and ecology of microorganisms, and how a good understanding of these microbial processes can enable beneficial applications of microorganisms in biotechnology and bioremediation.

ENVS*2090.title: Problem Solving in Environmental Biology
ENVS*2090.description: This course provides an introduction to current issues and problems in environmental biology. Approaches to characterizing and addressing problems through the systematic application of the scientific method will be critically examined. Students will have the opportunity to practice both oral and written presentation skills.

ENVS*2120.title: Introduction to Environmental Stewardship
ENVS*2120.description: This course provides an introduction to the concepts of resource management, environmental planning and assessment, land stewardship and sustainable development. Case studies of specific issues such as parks and natural heritage conservation, agricultural land loss, and integrated rural resources management will provide insight on approaches to decision making. Included will be discussion of the concept of stewardship as an environmental ethics.

ENVS*2130.title: Eating Sustainably in Ontario
ENVS*2130.description: This course is intended to introduce students to the science behind food related issues within the context of Ontario agriculture and food systems. The course will provide students with an overview of the history of agriculture in Ontario and an opportunity to discuss both sides of current debates over food production and associated environmental and human health issues.
ENVS*2130.restriction: Not available to students registered in the BAS, BBRM, BIESP, BSC, BSC(Agr) or BSC(Env) programs.

ENVS*2210.title: Apiculture and Honey Bee Biology
ENVS*2210.description: This course is designed to acquaint the student with the broad field of beekeeping. It will include honey bee biology and behaviour, management for honey production, products of the hive, pests and enemies and the value of bees as pollinators of agricultural crops.

ENVS*2230.title: Communications in Environmental Science
ENVS*2230.description: This course provides students with direct training in the academic skills used in researching and communicating environmental science. Within the context of current problems in environmental science, students will develop skills in library research, statistical interpretation, oral and poster presentation and written communication to diverse audiences. Students will research and report on scientific issues within environmental issues being reported in the media.
ENVS*2230.restriction: Registration in BBRM, BSC(Env) or BSC(Agr) programs.

ENVS*2240.title: Fundamentals of Environmental Geology
ENVS*2240.description: This course introduces the concepts and real-world examples of environmental issues related to plate tectonics, natural resources and igneous, metamorphic and sedimentary processes and rocks, groundwater and structural geology. Students will develop laboratory skills in rock and mineral identification, geological map interpretation and site characterization.

ENVS*2250.title: Geology of Natural Disasters
ENVS*2250.description: This course will offer insight into the mechanisms of natural geological disasters and their effects on Planet Earth, human civilization and life in general. Events before, during and after geological disasters such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, meteorite impact and climate change will be the focus of this course. This course will not count as a science credit for B.Sc. students.

ENVS*2270.title: Impacts of Climate Change
ENVS*2270.description: This course will provide students with an overview of recent research into what climate change means for Canada and Canadians. Students will learn about evidence for significant changes to the Boreal forests and about the potential impacts of climate change on human health through increasing heat waves and the heat stress on individuals. The course is intended to bridge the gap between abstract discussion of the climate and understand what these changes mean at both personal and societal levels.
ENVS*2270.restriction: Not available to students registered in the BAS, BBRM, BIESP, BSC, BSC(Agr) or BSC(Env) programs.

ENVS*2310.title: Introduction to Biogeochemistry
ENVS*2310.description: This course explores quantitatively and qualitatively how biological processes control element fluxes between water, air, and earth materials. Students will gain an understanding of the cycles for major elements (C, N, P, S etc.) in Earth's surface environments, including soil, rivers, lakes and oceans. Topics of current interest, such as resource extraction, climate change and geoengineering will be discussed in terms of their contributions to major element cycles. Seminars include quantitation exercises, hands-on exercises, and discussions to complement topics covered in the lectures.

ENVS*2330.title: Current Issues in Ecosystem Science and Biodiversity
ENVS*2330.description: This course provides an introduction to a range of specific environmental and scientific issues relating to ecological sciences. Issues to be covered include the biology of climate change, forest science and management of terrestrial ecosystems. Three examples of current problems of societal concern will be used as starting points to examine the role of science in addressing them, while developing students' knowledge of the underlying science and its relation to policy and economics.

prerequisites.raw: 1 of BIOL*1050, BIOL*1070, ENVM*1000, ENVM*1200, ENVS*1030

ENVS*2700.title: Introduction to Trees and Conservation
ENVS*2700.description: This course offers an introduction to scientific principles and nature through the University of Guelph's Arboretum. It explores natural history, tree life, and a wide range of conservation and community issues with local to global relevance. Designed for students of all backgrounds, the course covers the creation and care of a world-class Arboretum, tree and shrub identification, and the biodiversity they support. Students will examine tree conservation, human interventions, and the ecological roles of natural areas, while land-based learning highlights the health benefits of green spaces. Lectures, discussions, observations, and hands-on activities will deepen students' appreciation for Ontario's trees and their place in the natural world.

ENVS*2710.title: Bird Identification and Conservation
ENVS*2710.description: This course provides an engaging introduction to the bird life of southern Ontario, designed for students of all backgrounds, including those with no prior science experience. Students will learn how to identify bird species by sight and, for many, by sound. The course explores bird behavior, conservation, and research, highlighting the ecological roles of birds and their benefits to human well-being, including mental health. Through hands-on observation and discussion, students will gain a deeper appreciation for birds, their diversity in Ontario, and their place in the natural world.

ENVS*3000.title: Nature Interpretation
ENVS*3000.description: This course explores communication and experiential learning theories and their application to natural history interpretation and environmental education program design and delivery. Students will develop interpretive materials, plan an interpretive walk and deliver the interpretive walk to a community group.

ENVS*3010.title: Climate Change Biology
ENVS*3010.description: This course examines the impacts of climate change on living organisms, biological communities and ecosystems. The course focuses on what is known, and what is not known, about the ways in which the suite of changing climate variables influence biological systems.

ENVS*3020.title: Pesticides and the Environment
ENVS*3020.description: This course examines the role and use of pesticides by various facets of society and the effect of these pesticides on biological activities in the environment. Preparation of a research proposal is required for the course.

prerequisites.raw: [BIOL*1040 or (2 of BIOL*1050, BIOL*1070, BIOL*1080, BIOL*1090)], CHEM*1040

ENVS*3040.title: Natural Chemicals in the Environment
ENVS*3040.description: This course explores the roles of naturally occurring chemicals in the inter-relationships of organisms, and the historical and current uses of natural chemicals by humans for agricultural and medicinal purposes.

prerequisites.raw: BIOL*1040 or (2 of BIOL*1050, BIOL*1070, BIOL*1080, BIOL*1090)

ENVS*3050.title: Microclimatology
ENVS*3050.description: This course examines natural and intentionally-modified microclimates near the earth's surface; energy budgets; transport of mass and heat. Familiarization with some instruments for microclimatic measurements will be required.

ENVS*3060.title: Groundwater
ENVS*3060.description: This course provides a general understanding of the physical and chemical processes that operate in the groundwater zone under natural and human-induced conditions. The interrelations between the groundwater regime and the other components of the hydrological cycle are studied. Considerable emphasis is placed on the applied aspects of topics such as exploration, testing and development of aquifers for water supply, the chemical quality of groundwater, and the hydrogeological aspects of waste disposal.

ENVS*3080.title: Soil and Water Conservation
ENVS*3080.description: This course examines the processes leading to deterioration of soil and water quality, the impact of deterioration on use, and preventative or corrective measures: soil erosion by water and wind, soil compaction and salinization, drainage channel maintenance, sedimentation and nutrient enrichment of water, conservation programs and policies, and reclamation of severely disturbed soils and saline-sodic soils. Emphasis will be on concepts and solutions to problems in a systems approach.

ENVS*3090.title: Insect Diversity and Biology
ENVS*3090.description: This course is an overview of insect diversity and biology emphasizing groups of importance in conservation biology, outdoor recreation and economic entomology. Labs focus on insect identification and the development of a small insect collection.

prerequisites.raw: BIOL*1040 or (2 of BIOL*1050, BIOL*1070, BIOL*1080, BIOL*1090)

ENVS*3140.title: Management of Turfgrass Diseases
ENVS*3140.description: In this course ecology of turfgrass diseases and cultural methods of management will be emphasized, in addition to field recognition and microscopic diagnosis of diseases. Advances in biological and chemical control measures and their impact on turfgrass ecosystems and surrounding environments will also be discussed.

ENVS*3150.title: Aquatic Systems
ENVS*3150.description: In this course students will be taught how to apply quantitative methods to the analysis of aquatic systems of the earth from many simultaneous perspectives. The material will include the physical, chemical and biological components of the various liquid surficial structures and processes and also how they interact with humans. The economic, social and policy implications of humans interacting with aquatic systems will also be emphasized. The history of the analysis of aquatic systems will be systematically included in the material.

prerequisites.raw: 10.00 credits including (BIOL*1030, BIOL*1040) or (BIOL*1070, BIOL*1090), CHEM*1040

ENVS*3180.title: Sedimentary Environments
ENVS*3180.description: This course examines the principles of sedimentology and stratigraphy as applied to various ancient and modern sedimentary systems. Students will learn to describe and interpret sedimentary deposits in terrestrial and marine systems as well as the larger forces that control the preservation and evolution of these sedimentary systems over geological time. The course includes several field trips.

ENVS*3210.title: Plant Pathology
ENVS*3210.description: This course examines the nature of disease in plants, including their causal agents, etiology, biology, epidemiology, and management. Emphasis is placed on the historical and social importance of plant diseases, and on current issues in plant health.

ENVS*3220.title: Terrestrial Chemistry
ENVS*3220.description: Chemical reactions within Earth surface environments are vital to element cycling, bioavailability and the remediation of contaminated environments. Soil and saturated sediments (wetlands, lakes, and rivers) are particularly vital reactors for chemical processes that move elements from rocks and minerals to water, biota and the atmosphere. Students in the course explore the major processes governing reactions in soils and freshwater sediments and apply their knowledge to understand contamination and remediation in environments such as legacy mines, acid- and salt-affected soil, as well as the chemical basis for soil productivity.

ENVS*3230.title: Agroforestry Systems
ENVS*3230.description: The planned and systematic integration of trees into the agricultural landscape can potentially result in sustainable environmental, ecological, economic and social benefits. The key aspects of deriving these benefits, associated science and management considerations, application potentials at the landscape level and adoption challenges will be discussed. Common temperate and tropical agroforestry systems (e.g. intercropping of trees and crops) will be discussed. Emphasis will be given to successful research and development case studies.

ENVS*3240.title: Creative Writing for Environmental Science
ENVS*3240.description: Science is inherently a creative endeavour and scientists are increasingly being called upon to communicate their ideas and findings to new audiences. In this interdisciplinary course, we will examine texts of creative writing for the environmental science presented within, following up with reading scientific articles related to the subject matter. We will examine the role that creative writing (poetry, storytelling, essays and more) can play in creating inspiration, interest, and even rigour in scientific thinking and communication. By the end of the course, students will be able to interpret and appraise the capacity of the creative process to unfold scientific discoveries, apply techniques of creative writing, and explore the fundamentals of an environmental science topic.
ENVS*3240.restriction: Restricted to students in BSES, BBRM.EM or BSCH.ENVB.

ENVS*3250.title: Forest Health and Disease
ENVS*3250.description: The impact of beneficial and pathogenic microorganisms on forest health, and the biology and management of tree diseases in natural and urban ecosystems is covered in this course. Emphasis will be placed on ecological processes, host-pathogen interactions, mutualistic associations, wood decay, and human impacts on tree health.

ENVS*3270.title: Forest Biodiversity
ENVS*3270.description: This course examines biodiversity in forest ecosystems at a variety of scales from genes to landscapes. Relationships between biodiversity and forest ecosystem structure, function, and stability are explored. Approaches to conserving biodiversity in managed forests are discussed and evaluated. Analysis of the relevant scientific literature and practical experience with methods of quantifying biodiversity are emphasized in the weekly seminar.

ENVS*3290.title: Waterborne Disease Ecology
ENVS*3290.description: This course examines emerging and re-emerging waterborne diseases (bacterial, protozoan, and viral) as a function of environmental change (including chemical and biological pollution and climate change). Waterborne diseases, in freshwater and marine ecosystems, will be examined from historical and contemporary issues as they relate to public and environmental health from regional, national, and international perspectives. Topics presented within the course will include current waterborne diseases of humans and aquatic fauna, detection of waterborne pathogens, microbial evolution, microbial physiology, water regulations and protection of drinking water.

ENVS*3300.title: Introduction to Controlled Environment Systems
ENVS*3300.description: Controlled Environment Systems (CES), otherwise known as Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA), is a rapidly evolving technological framework for producing crops in a stable, controlled and efficient manner. This CES/CEA technological platform is poised to play a significant role in current and emerging social and environmental issues including, but not limited to, climate change adaptation, food insecurity (urban and Northern/remote communities), local food supply, food supply chain stabilization, food safety, medicinal crop production and standardization, biodiversity preservation, functional foods/improved human nutrition, etc. Highly controlled production of plants in CES is even an absolute requirement in the global quest to explore and colonize the moon, Mars and beyond. This course will provide the student with a strong understanding of the principles of CES/CEA and will dig deep into the wide range of commercial, research, and human space exploration applications of CES/CEA. Through numerous guest speakers, students will also develop a sense of the career opportunities in this rapidly growing sector.

ENVS*3310.title: Soil Biodiversity and Ecosystem Function
ENVS*3310.description: Soils are the site of complex interactions between minerals, water, air, organic matter and living organisms. This course will focus on the organisms that live in the soil and their activities in soil ecosystems, soil as a habitat for organisms, the key role of microorganisms in nutrient cycles and plant-microbe relationships and will review basic soil microbial and ecological principles.

prerequisites.raw: 10.00 credits including (1 of AGR*2320, ENVS*2060, SOIL*2010)

ENVS*3340.title: Environmental Data Analysis
ENVS*3340.description: This course is focused on interpreting data of the physical environment. Students will access various online databases, such as meteorological and hydrological time series, and perform and interpret statistical analysis with the data. Issues around errors and data collection will be explored by performing simple experiments. Students will make a numerical simulation of a dynamic environmental phenomenon. Students will produce and interpret graphical presentations of data.

prerequisites.raw: 1 of ECON*2740, GEOG*2460, STAT*2040, STAT*2060, STAT*2230

ENVS*3370.title: Terrestrial Ecosystem Ecology
ENVS*3370.description: In this course, the ecosystem is the biological level of organization of interest. Living organisms and their physical environment are considered as components of an integrated system. Key ecosystem processes such as energy flow, carbon and nutrient cycling, and succession, and the controls on these processes, are examined. The impacts of human activity and global change on ecosystem structure and function are investigated.

ENVS*4000.title: Toxicological Risk Assessment
ENVS*4000.description: This advanced course will give students a working knowledge of current processes and techniques for toxicological risk characterization. The course material will cover problem definition, effect characterization, exposure characterization, risk assessment and risk management decision making.

ENVS*4001.title: Project in Environmental Sciences
ENVS*4001.description: First part of the two-semester course ENVS*4001/2. The course permits the student the opportunity to integrate both the skills and knowledge acquired in earlier courses through application to current environmental problems and issues. Group research problems and exposure to critical environmental policy issues will form the core elements of the course. This is a two-semester course offered over consecutive semesters. When you select it you must select ENVS*4001 in the Fall semester and ENVS*4002 in the Winter semester. A grade will not be assigned to ENVS*4001 until ENVS*4002 is completed.
ENVS*4001.restriction: 12.00 credits. Registration in the BSC(Env) or BSCH.ENVB programs.

ENVS*4002.title: Project in Environmental Sciences
ENVS*4002.description: This is the second part of the two-semester course ENVS*4001/2. The course permits the student the opportunity to integrate both the skills and knowledge acquired in earlier courses through application to current environmental problems and issues. Group research problems and exposure to critical environmental policy issues will form the core elements of the course. This is a two-semester course offered over consecutive semesters. When you select it you must select ENVS*4001 in the Fall semester and ENVS*4002 in the Winter semester. A grade will not be assigned to ENVS*4001 until ENVS*4002 is completed.

ENVS*4030.title: Ecohydrology
ENVS*4030.description: This course introduces the emerging discipline of ecohydrology, which focuses on interactions and feedbacks between the elements of the water cycle, ecosystems and organisms. The emphasis will be on how hydrological processes regulate ecological ones but also on the mechanisms of ecological regulation of hydrological processes - which are often ignored in traditional hydrologic investigations. Students will learn about complex dynamics prevailing in ecohydrological systems using a blend of theory, quantitative tools and case studies. Students will also learn to synthesize recent literature, collect and analyze relevant data from open-access databases, and perform holistic ecohydrological system analysis through a term project.

prerequisites.raw: (BIOL*3130 or GEOG*3610), (1 of GEOG*2460, STAT*2040, STAT*2060, STAT*2120, STAT*2230)

ENVS*4050.title: Predicting Impacts of Environmental Change
ENVS*4050.description: This course is focused on the effects of urbanization and pollutant and greenhouse gas emissions on local, regional and global climates and the subsequent impacts on human life. Students will develop and apply simple numerical models of atmospheric processes toward solving practical problems, including applications related to land-atmosphere exchange of heat and water, air quality, and assessment of thermal comfort in urban environments.
