SOC*4150.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP, BAH.CJPP:C and BAH.SOC with an average of 70% in all course attempts in Sociology and Sociology and Anthropology courses. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4170.title: Violence Against Women and Gendered Violence
SOC*4170.description: This course examines the premise that violence against women and gendered violence are the products of historical and institutional inequalities linked to structural and symbolic processes, and will pay attention to the role of gender not as an identity, but as a social structure that intersects with other structures of inequality.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (SOC*2700 or SOC*3310)

SOC*4170.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP, BAH.CJPP:C and BAH.SOC with an average of 70% in all course attempts in Sociology and Sociology and Anthropology courses. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4200.title: Advanced Topics in Criminal Justice
SOC*4200.description: This is an in-depth study of issues in criminal justice.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (SOAN*3120 or POLS*3650)

SOC*4200.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH:CJPP and BAH:CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4280.title: Climate Crisis and Environmental Justice
SOC*4280.description: This seminar-based course explores the climate crisis and environmental justice through a socio-ecological lens. Each offering centers on a specific environmental theme selected by the instructor-such as water, food systems, land, energy, or broader human-nature relationships- while maintaining a core emphasis on justice, sustainability, and climate resilience. Drawing on diverse knowledge systems, the course examines how colonialism, capitalism, and systemic inequality shape socio- ecological futures. Case studies from Turtle Island (i.e., areas currently known as Canada) and global contexts highlight the lived experiences and leadership of frontline communities.

SOC*4300.title: Sociology of Everyday Life
SOC*4300.description: This seminar-based course draws on data from everyday life to consider a range of approaches to integrating theory and methods. Students will engage with current theoretical and methodological developments in the discipline to examine relationships between the realities of everyday experience and the more abstract social structures that shape our lives.

SOC*4320.title: Advanced Seminar in Criminal Justice
SOC*4320.description: This seminar offers an in-depth examination of selected issues and debates in criminal justice. The specific focus of the seminar will vary based on the expertise of the instructor teaching the seminar and contemporary social and cultural debates.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4320.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4330.title: Miscarriages of Justice
SOC*4330.description: This course will explore procedures used by various criminal justice actors to assess their potential for producing errors in justice, consider the implications of balancing the rights of the accused with crime control, and discuss existing redress mechanisms to remedy miscarriages of justice and provide suggestions for policy change. Students will consider the psychological, familial, emotional, and economic consequences of wrongful convictions.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4330.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4340.title: Women and the Criminal Justice System
SOC*4340.description: This course will critically examine selective issues related to the way women (including young women) as offenders, victims/survivors, and practitioners/professionals in the criminal justice system are theorized, researched, represented in media and attended to in policy and practice. Students will consider the role of age, race, class, gender, abilities, sexualities and the media in the shaping of public opinion and public policy about women and crime.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4340.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4350.title: Drugs and Society
SOC*4350.description: This course will examine sociological perspectives on substance use behaviour and related policy discussions about drugs. Students will examine theoretical and substantive contributions on an array of topics- from the concept of addiction to historical and recent experiences of users of a variety of drugs.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4350.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4410.title: Women, Work and Public Policy
SOC*4410.description: In this course students will critically assess the transformation of women's work in contemporary society. A range of topics pertaining to women's work will be explored with particular attention paid to the processes through which class, gender, race, ethnicity, and age shape divisions of work. The course will also focus on theories that have attempted to explain the transformation of women's work.

prerequisites.raw: 12.50 credits including (1 of ANTH*2160, ANTH*2180, IDEV*2000, SOAN*2112, SOC*2700), (1 of GEOG*2260, POLS*2650, SOAN*2120)

SOC*4420.title: Food, Society and Sustainability
SOC*4420.description: This seminar explores food systems in the contemporary world and how food intersects with issues such as climate change and social justice. The course considers different food system actors (e.g., farmers, citizens, corporations, governments) and examines how power shapes mainstream food production and consumption, and highlights examples of more just, sustainable alternatives.

prerequisites.raw: 12.50 credits including (1 of GEOG*2260, IDEV*2100, POLS*2650, SOAN*2120, SOAN*3070), (1 of ANTH*2160, ANTH*2180, IDEV*1100, IDEV*2000, IDEV*2300, SOAN*2110, SOAN*2112)

SOC*4430.title: Alternative Social Possibilities
SOC*4430.description: This course considers the role of idealism, hope, and utopia within social research and theory. Students will examine the relationship between creativity and social change, and the different ways individuals, groups and organizations imagine a better world to implement progressive changes. Students will evaluate different case studies and debates on how to build a just society, reflect on knowledge creation and action, and develop a multi-stage project.

prerequisites.raw: 12.50 credits including (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (1 of POLS*2650, POLS*3180, SOAN*2120)

SOC*4460.title: Advanced Seminar in Criminology
SOC*4460.description: This advanced seminar offers an in-depth study of selected issues in criminology.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4460.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4470.title: Youth in Conflict with the Law
SOC*4470.description: This course offers an in-depth, critical analysis of youth crime and deviance. The purpose of the course is to provide students with an up-to- date overview of sociological theory and empirical research in the field of youth crime and social/criminal justice policy.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4470.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4480.title: Moral Regulation
SOC*4480.description: This course introduces students to contemporary literature and critical perspectives on the evolving concept of moral regulation by examining theoretical and substantive contributions, with particular attention to Canadian examples. Potential topics of discussion include sexual behaviour, drug consumption, public health, and the moral regulation of the poor.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4480.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4490.title: Advanced Theory and Methods in Criminology
SOC*4490.description: This course is designed to teach students how to test criminological theory with self-report survey data. Special attention will be given to the conceptual logic involved in linking theory with data, in addition to understanding the technical skills that are required to undertake this approach to criminological research.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4490.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4500.title: Crime and Social Exclusion
SOC*4500.description: This course is designed to introduce students to the ways in which socially excluded populations (e.g., homeless people) encounter the criminal justice system. Students will review a range of topics, statistical trends, and theory and research on the ways in which socially excluded groups come into contact with the police, courts and correctional system.

prerequisites.raw: 14.00 credits including (POLS*3650 or SOAN*3120), (1 of ANTH*3690, SOC*2700, SOC*3310), (2 of SOC*3490, SOC*3710, SOC*3720, SOC*3730, SOC*3740, SOC*3750)

SOC*4500.restriction: Restricted to students in BAH.CJPP and BAH.CJPP:C. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4740.title: Seminar in Sociology
SOC*4740.description: This course will be offered as a structured seminar on various topics depending upon the interests of the faculty member teaching the course. Topics will be announced and course outlines will be available at course selection. The availability of third and fourth year seminar courses will vary. Students must check with the Department of Sociology and Anthropology to see when seminar courses are available.

SOC*4840.title: Seminar in Sociology
SOC*4840.description: This course will be offered as a structured seminar on various topics depending upon the interests of the faculty member teaching the course. Topics will be announced and course outlines will be available at course selection. The availability of third and fourth year seminar courses will vary. Students must check with the Department of Sociology and Anthropology to see when seminar courses are available.

SOC*4880.title: Special Projects in Sociology
SOC*4880.description: This special study/reading course option is designed to provide advanced undergraduates with an opportunity to explore independently the frontiers and foundations of a field of knowledge. Under supervision, the student will study in greater depth topics related to regular upper- level courses offered in the department which the student has taken or is taking. Permission of the instructor who will be supervising the project is required.
SOC*4880.restriction: Instructor consent required. Please note, a student is allowed a total of 1.00 credits only for reading courses.

SOC*4890.title: Special Projects in Sociology
SOC*4890.description: This special study/reading course option is designed to provide advanced undergraduates with an opportunity to explore independently the frontiers and foundations of a field of knowledge. Under supervision, the student will study in greater depth topics related to regular upper- level courses offered in the department which the student has taken or is taking. Permission of the instructor who will be supervising the project is required.
SOC*4890.restriction: Instructor consent required. Please note, a student is allowed a total of 1.00 credits only for reading courses.

SOC*4900.title: Honours Sociology Thesis I
SOC*4900.description: Development and design of an honours thesis proposal conducted under the supervision of a faculty member. Recommended to Honours students.

prerequisites.raw: 15.00 credits including SOC*3310, SOAN*3070, SOAN*3120. CJPP students must have 15.00 credits including SOC*2700, SOAN*3120, or POLS*3650

SOC*4900.restriction: A cumulative average of 75% in all Sociology and Anthropology courses. Instructor consent required. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SOC*4910.title: Honours Sociology Thesis II
SOC*4910.description: Completion and presentation of honours thesis.
SOC*4910.restriction: Instructor consent required. CJPP and CJPP:C students are restricted to a maximum of 3.00 credits in SOC and POLS 4000-level CJPP restricted electives.

SPAN*1100.title: Introductory Spanish I
SPAN*1100.description: This course introduces students to the basics of spoken and written Spanish through the study of grammar, vocabulary and culture. The course is for students with no previous studies in Spanish.
SPAN*1100.restriction: This course is not intended for students with prior knowledge in Spanish. Students with previous knowledge in the language must seek advice on course selection from the Head of Spanish and Hispanic Studies and might be assigned to another level of Spanish.

SPAN*1110.title: Introductory Spanish II
SPAN*1110.description: This is a continuation of SPAN*1100. Students will develop and apply increased reading, writing, listening and oral skills in Spanish through the study of new vocabulary, grammatical structures and culture.

SPAN*1500.title: Hispanic Film
SPAN*1500.description: This course, taught in English, introduces students to cinema in the Hispanic world. Students will examine major works in the Hispanic cinematic tradition. Depending on the instructor, Spanish-language films from Spain, South America, Central America, the Caribbean, the United States, the Philippines or Africa may be included. Themes discussed will include the historical and artistic contexts in which the films were released, the economic and political difficulties facing film production and distribution, censorship, competition from Hollywood, concepts of genre, ideological commitment and the role of the director as well as issues such as poverty, migration, ethnic difference, political conflict and the roles of women and sexual and gender minorities.

SPAN*2000.title: Intermediate Spanish I
SPAN*2000.description: This course is for students who have completed either first year University Spanish or 4U Spanish as well as for those who are heritage speakers. This course examines cultural topics and grammatical structures in greater depth while focusing on vocabulary acquisition, and development of oral, written, and listening skills.

SPAN*2010.title: Intermediate Spanish II
SPAN*2010.description: Students will acquire and apply improved skills in reading, writing, and conversation through further study of grammar and culture. The course encourages students to enhance their analytical and linguistic skills through the use of appropriate structures as they formulate and support opinions.

SPAN*2040.title: Culture of Spain
SPAN*2040.description: This course is an examination of the historical and cultural events that provided the background for the development of modern Spain, as well as a visual survey of Spanish culture.

SPAN*2990.title: Hispanic Literary Studies
SPAN*2990.description: This course is an introduction to literary studies in Spanish. The course focuses on critical terminology and methods through a selection of prose, poetry and drama from Spain and Spanish America.

SPAN*3080.title: Spanish American Culture
SPAN*3080.description: This course is a survey through selected readings, class discussion and audio-visual materials of the Spanish American countries, their histories, society, institutions and culture.

SPAN*3210.title: Topics in Hispanic Studies
SPAN*3210.description: This course, taught in Spanish, with texts in the original language, provides an intensive study of a specific aspect of Hispanic Studies.

SPAN*3220.title: Literature and Arts I: Spain
SPAN*3220.description: This course will be taught in Spanish, with texts studied in the original language, and provides a detailed examination of a theme, period or movement in the literature and/or arts of Spain.

SPAN*3230.title: Literature and Arts II: Latin America
SPAN*3230.description: This course will be taught in Spanish, with texts studied in the original language, and provides a detailed examination of a theme, period or movement in the literature and/or arts of Latin America.

SPAN*3240.title: Topics in Hispanic Linguistics
SPAN*3240.description: This course offers an introduction to linguistic topics including history of the Spanish language, language variation and change, and Spanish phonetics, which may vary from year to year.

SPAN*3500.title: Advanced Spanish I
SPAN*3500.description: This is an advanced language course that focuses on the refinement of students' written and verbal communication skills in Spanish.

SPAN*3700.title: Experiential Learning and Language
SPAN*3700.description: This course provides an opportunity for independent study based on an experiential project in Spanish or Hispanic Studies. The project (approximately 70 hours) must be approved by a faculty member in the School of Languages and Literatures. It will include research about experiential learning, a reflective piece of writing and a public oral presentation about the project.
SPAN*3700.restriction: A minimum cumulative average of 70% in all Spanish course attempts. Instructor consent required.

SPAN*3800.title: Directed Readings in Hispanic Studies
SPAN*3800.description: A reading course in Spanish or Spanish American literature designed according to the previous studies and the interests of the individual student. Normally, students will not be permitted to take more than two courses in the Directed Readings sequence.

prerequisites.raw: 1.00 credits from the following: (HISP*3220 or SPAN*3220), (HISP*3230 or SPAN*3230), SPAN*3080

SPAN*3810.title: Directed Readings in Hispanic Studies
SPAN*3810.description: A reading course in Spanish or Spanish American literature designed according to the previous studies and the interests of the individual student. Normally, students will not be permitted to take more than two courses in the Directed Readings sequence.

prerequisites.raw: 1.00 credits from the following: (HISP*3220 or SPAN*3220), (HISP*3230 or SPAN*3230), SPAN*3080

SPAN*4100.title: Seminar in Hispanic Studies
SPAN*4100.description: This seminar is taught in Spanish, with texts studied in the original language. It provides an opportunity for students to study and research a specific aspect of Hispanic Studies.

SPAN*4410.title: Senior Seminar on Latin America
SPAN*4410.description: This capstone seminar is taught in Spanish, with texts studied in the original language, and will explore in detail a major movement, period or theme in Latin American literature or culture. Students who have completed this course previously should register in SPAN*4100.

SPAN*4420.title: Senior Seminar on Spain or Africa
SPAN*4420.description: This seminar is taught in Spanish, with readings from texts in the original language. It addresses a major issue, movement, theme or period in the literature and culture of Spain, or, in some years, of the Spanish-speaking regions of Africa.

SPAN*4500.title: Spanish Translation - Theory and Practice
SPAN*4500.description: This course introduces students to the theory and practice of translation by applying current theoretical principles and linguistic precision to the process of translation from Spanish to English and English to Spanish. Students work with a variety of texts from multiple fields, including journalism, business, science and literature.

SPAN*4840.title: Research Paper in Hispanic Studies
SPAN*4840.description: Students will write an intensive research paper in Spanish on a topic relating to a literary or linguistic subject in Hispanic Studies. This paper will be completed within one semester and students will be assigned to a faculty supervisor who will oversee the project.

SPMT*1020.title: Sport Management Today
SPMT*1020.description: This course provides an overview of sport management and leadership concepts and an introduction to the different contexts of sport including the youth, community and scholastic sport and recreation, professional, and international sport. Topics covered include the history of sport management, the relevance and practice of managerial and leadership concepts within sport, diversity and ethical issues in sport, and sustainability practices in sport.
SPMT*1020.restriction: Not available to students registered in BCOM.SPMT, BCOM.SPMT:C, BCOM.SPMT:O and MIN.SPMT programs.

SPMT*1120.title: Dynamics of Sport Fans
SPMT*1120.description: This course delves into the intricacies of understanding and engaging consumers within the dynamic landscape of the sports industry. Students will explore the fundamental principles and theories that underpin consumer behavior, applying them specifically to the context of sports. Key topics include the psychological and sociological factors influencing sport consumption, including motivation, identity, and social influences. Students will analyze sport consumer activity and engagement through the lens of fandom, examining the emotional and cognitive connections that individuals develop with sport activities, athletes, teams, and brands. The course will also address the unique characteristics of sport fandom, such as loyalty and brand allegiance, providing a basic understanding of what makes sport fandom so interesting, distinct, and lucrative.
SPMT*1120.restriction: Not available to students registered in BCOM.SPMT, BCOM.SPMT:C, BCOM.SPMT:O and MIN.SPMT programs.

SPMT*2020.title: The Business of Sport and Event Tourism
SPMT*2020.description: This course provides an introduction to the multi-faceted sports and event tourism industry from a business perspective. It focusses on a range of major sporting events such as the Olympics, national and international championships, and their influence on the sport tourism market in Canada. Students will understand components of sport management, event logistics, sponsorship, hospitality and licensing agreements.
SPMT*2020.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information.

SPMT*2120.title: Understanding Sport Fandom
SPMT*2120.description: This course will introduce students to the science of fandom, equipping them with an in-depth understanding of fan perspectives, attitudes and affinities. Students will explore the psychology, sociology and neuroscience of sport fandom. Psychological frameworks related to social group dynamics and fan communities will be examined, as well as current insights related to the motivations and influences that shape different fan preferences and behaviours. Implications will be drawn relative to developing cutting-edge fan engagement and revenue generation strategies across the sport ecosystem.

SPMT*2220.title: Communication and Media Strategy in Sport and Events
SPMT*2220.description: All successful communication and media strategies in sport and events must take into account the complexities of this diverse and dynamic market of sport-related goods and services. This course examines sport and event communications through the investigation of internal and external factors that shape consumer demand in related industry sectors with an emphasis on digital media applications. Specifically, it will analyze the social, cultural and economic factors influencing the marketing of products, services and ideas.
SPMT*2220.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Restricted to students in BCOMM.SPMT, BCOMM.SPMT:C, and BCOMM.SPMT:O. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information.

SPMT*3020.title: The Impact of Business on Sport Industry
SPMT*3020.description: To understand the role of a sports manager, students will apply financial decision making concepts to evaluate the continually evolving sports industry. Students will explore how globalization trends, technological advancements, and demographic changes are impacting the sports industry and their effect on supply, demand, pricing, commercialization, labour policy, and revenue in the sports industry.
SPMT*3020.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information.

SPMT*3220.title: Sales, Sponsorship and Stakeholder Engagement in Sport
SPMT*3220.description: This course introduces key tactical applications and practical concepts for future professionals in sport management and covers the major topics of sales, sponsorship and stakeholder engagement in the sport and event industry. The topic of sales includes learning sales techniques and processes related to tickets, suites, sponsorship, merchandise, and other products in the industry. The course delves deeper into sponsorship to build an understanding of this promotional tool. This course addresses engagement as one of the key success factors in the sport business today.
SPMT*3220.restriction: Restricted to students in BCOMM.SPMT, BCOM.SPMT:C and BCOM.SPMT:O. This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. Please contact the department for more information.

SPMT*3400.title: Current Topics in Sport Management
SPMT*3400.description: This course provides a lecture, discussion or seminar program on a selected topic or current issue in sport. The specific topic of the course will be identified on an ad hoc basis as contemporary issues and topics in sport arise.
SPMT*3400.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. See department for more information.

SPMT*4020.title: Advanced Concepts in Sport and Event Management
SPMT*4020.description: Students will apply advanced sport and event business concepts in an interactive setting, using principles and concepts of sport and event management to develop strategic planning and organizational skills. The course applies these concepts towards effective decision-making for stakeholders in relation to financial resource management, contracting, networks, digital elements, revenue generation, operations and emerging issues in the sport and event industry.
SPMT*4020.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Restricted to students in BCOMM.SPMT, BCOMM.SPMT:C and BCOMM.SPMT:O Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. Please contact the department for more information.

SPMT*4040.title: Advanced Topics in Sport Management
SPMT*4040.description: This advanced topic course introduces students to the vast, growing and varied literature and conceptual foundations directly and indirectly associated with the field of sport management. This course exposes students to the current and seminal literature relevant to the current trends of the sport industry to provide an understanding of the key issues and gaps to be addressed in future research and the overall challenges facing the field.
SPMT*4040.restriction: Restricted to students in BCOM.SPMT, BCOM.SPMT:C and BCOM.SPMT:ODepartment(s):Department of Management

SPMT*4080.title: Experiential Learning and Leadership in Sport Management
SPMT*4080.description: This project-based, experiential learning course is restricted to fourth-year Sport and Event Management majors. This course provides students with practical experience and prepares them for various elements in the sport sector by providing them with tangible, hands-on experience within the sport industry.
SPMT*4080.restriction: Restricted to students in BCOM.SPMT and BCOM.SPMT:C. Instructor consent is required

SPMT*4140.title: Advanced Sport Analytics
SPMT*4140.description: This course applies an evidence-based approach to decision-making using analytic tools to transform data into insight. Students will use existing math, statistics and business skills and knowledge to address business problems within the sports industry. While the use and application of business analytics will be addressed with respect to both on-field and off-field, this course will focus on on-field analytics (e.g., measuring and predicting player and team performance).
SPMT*4140.restriction: Restricted to students in BCOM.SPMT, BCOM.SPMT:C and BCOM.SPMT:O.

STAT*1200.title: Probability and Chance
STAT*1200.description: This introductory course in probability includes topics such as: the nature of chance and randomness, philosophy and history of efforts to quantify uncertainty, elementary probability including conditional probability, combinatorics, empirical probability, games of chance, probability paradoxes, probability distributions, simulation, and perceptions of probability by biological organisms. Probability is treated both as a key mathematical discipline and as foundational for further studies in statistics and data science.

prerequisites.raw: 1 of 4U Advanced Functions, 4U Calculus, or equivalent

STAT*1200.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to the BMath program during certain periods.

STAT*2040.title: Statistics I
STAT*2040.description: This course focuses on the practical methods of statistics. Topics will include: descriptive statistics; probability; discrete and continuous random variables and their probability distributions, including the binomial, Poisson, and normal distributions; sampling distributions; statistical inference including confidence intervals and hypothesis tests; one- and two-sample inference procedures; one-way analysis of variance; chi-square tests; simple linear regression and correlation. Assignments will deal with real data from the natural sciences and involve the use of statistical software for computing and visualization.

prerequisites.raw: 1 of 4U Calculus and Vectors, Advanced Functions and Calculus, OAC Calculus, MATH*1080

STAT*2040.restriction: STAT*2060, STAT*2080, STAT*2120, STAT*2230. This is a Priority Access Course. Enrollment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information.

STAT*2050.title: Statistics II
STAT*2050.description: In this course, students will learn how to implement good study design and analyze data from complex studies. This course follows naturally from STAT*2040 and features both previously unseen statistical techniques, as well as studying in greater depth some topics covered in STAT*2040. These topics will include: experiments and observational studies; a review of t-tests and confidence intervals; confounding variables; association and causality; Analysis of Variance (ANOVA); simple and multiple linear regression; binary responses (logistic regression); odds ratios and relative risk; and an introduction to experimental design (including blocked designs and factorial treatment designs). Assignments carried out using modern statistical software will form the basis for mastering the material.

STAT*2060.title: Statistics for Business Decisions
STAT*2060.description: This course is designed for students interested in the application of statistics in a business setting. Topics will include graphical and numerical methods for describing various data types, including time series data; basic probability; discrete and continuous probability distributions; sampling distributions; confidence intervals and hypothesis testing for one- and two-sample problems; and linear regression and correlation. The role of statistics in business decisions will be discussed throughout the course, and industry-relevant software will be used for data visualization and computation.

prerequisites.raw: (4U mathematics or equivalent) or 0.50 credit in mathematics

STAT*2060.restriction: STAT*2040, STAT*2080, STAT*2120, STAT*2230. Not available to BSC students.

STAT*2080.title: Introductory Applied Statistics I
STAT*2080.description: The topics covered in this course include: Frequency distributions, graphing and tabulation of data; measures of central tendency, variability and association; elementary probability; hypothesis testing and confidence intervals; basic concepts of experimental design; treatment designs; simple linear regression and correlation. Examples come from a variety of disciplines, including family studies, education, marketing, medicine, psychology and sociology.

prerequisites.raw: (4U mathematics or equivalent) or 0.50 credit in mathematics

STAT*2080.restriction: STAT*2040, STAT*2060, STAT*2120, STAT*2230. BSC students cannot take this course for credit.

STAT*2090.title: Introductory Applied Statistics II
STAT*2090.description: The topics covered in this course include: analysis of qualitative data; analysis of variance for designed experiments; multiple regression; exposure to non-parametric methods; power and sample size calculations; special topics such as logistic regression. Examples come from a variety of disciplines, including nutrition, family studies, education, marketing, medicine, psychology and sociology.

STAT*2120.title: Probability and Statistics for Engineers
STAT*2120.description: The topics covered in this course include: Sample spaces; probability, conditional probability and independence; Bayes' theorem; probability distributions; probability densities; algebra of expected values; descriptive statistics; inferences concerning means, variances, and proportions; curve fitting, the method of least squares and correlation. An introduction to quality control and reliability is provided. This course is recommended for students in the B.Sc.(Eng.) program.
STAT*2120.restriction: STAT*2040, STAT*2060, STAT*2080, STAT*2230. This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information

STAT*2230.title: Biostatistics for Integrative Biology
STAT*2230.description: This course introduces students to the design, completion and interpretation of research projects, including identifying categories of research questions, types of data, data gathering methods, efficient graphic and numeric methods to summarize data, standard statistical analyses involving parameter estimation and hypothesis tests and interpreting results in the context of research goals. Statistical concepts underlying practical aspects of biological research will be emphasized. Computer-intensive laboratory sessions will focus on practical data organization, visualization, statistical analysis using software, and interpretation and communication of statistical results.
STAT*2230.restriction: STAT*2040, STAT*2060, STAT*2080, STAT*2120. Restricted to students in BOH, BOH:C, BSC majors in BIOD, MFB, MFB:C, WBC, WBC:C, and ZOO, and BSES majors in ECOL and ECOL:C.
STAT*2230.department: Department of Mathematics and Statistics, Department of Integrative Biology

STAT*3100.title: Introductory Mathematical Statistics I
STAT*3100.description: The topics covered in this course include: Probability spaces; discrete and continuous random variables; multivariate distributions; expectations; moments, Chebyshev's inequality, product moments; sums of random variables, generating functions; Gamma, Beta, t and F distributions; central limit theorem; sampling distributions.

prerequisites.raw: (1 of IPS*1510, MATH*1090, MATH*1210, MATH*2080), (STAT*2040 or STAT*2120)

STAT*3110.title: Introductory Mathematical Statistics II
STAT*3110.description: Estimation, unbiasedness, Cramer-Rao inequality, consistency, sufficiency, method of moments, maximum likelihood estimation; hypothesis testing, Neyman-Pearson lemma, likelihood ratio test, uniformly most powerful test; linear regression and correlation; non- parametric methods.

STAT*3210.title: Experimental Design
STAT*3210.description: This course presents the basic principles of design: randomization, replication, and local control (blocking); RCBD, Latin square and crossover designs, incomplete block designs, factorial and split-plot experiments, confounding and fractional factorial designs, response surface methodology; linear mixed model computer analysis of the designs; nonparametric methods; Taguchi philosophy.

STAT*3240.title: Applied Regression Analysis
STAT*3240.description: This course reviews simple linear regression and introduces multiple regression with emphasis on theory of least squares estimation, residual analysis, and model interpretation. Within the multiple regression context, transformations of variables, interactions, model selection techniques, ANOVA, influence diagnostics and multicollinearity will be discussed. Topics may also include Box-Cox transformations, weighted regression, and logistic and Poisson regression. This course is supplemented with computer labs involving interactive data analysis using statistical software.

STAT*3320.title: Sampling Theory with Applications
STAT*3320.description: This course focuses on the design and analysis of survey samples for finite populations. Topics covered include: non-probability and probability sampling, simple random sampling, stratified sampling, cluster sampling, systematic sampling, double sampling, two-phase sampling and multi- stage cluster sampling. Expectation, variance estimation procedures and sample size calculations for the above techniques are included.

STAT*3510.title: Environmental Risk Assessment
STAT*3510.description: Contemporary statistical methods for assessing risk are discussed. Topics covered include: dose-response models, survival analysis, relative risk analysis, bioassay, estimating methods for zero risk, trend analysis, survey of models for assessing risk. Case studies are used to illustrate the methods.

STAT*4000.title: Statistical Computing
STAT*4000.description: The topics in this course will include pseudorandom number generation, numerical optimization as used in statistics, simulation study design, Monte Carlo integration and variance reduction, and bootstrapping. Other topics may include permutation tests, visualization of multivariate data, and big data.

STAT*4050.title: Topics in Applied Statistics
STAT*4050.description: Topics such as statistical computing procedures, quality control, bioassay, survival analysis and introductory stochastic processes will be covered. This course is intended for statistics students and interested students from other disciplines who have appropriate previous courses in statistics. Information on particular offerings will be available at the beginning of each academic year.

STAT*4060.title: Topics in Applied Statistics
STAT*4060.description: Topics such as statistical computing procedures, quality control, bioassay, survival analysis and introductory stochastic processes will be covered. This course is intended for statistics students and interested students from other disciplines who have appropriate previous courses in statistics. Information on particular offerings will be available at the beginning of each academic year.

STAT*4150.title: Topics in Applied Statistics
STAT*4150.description: Topics such as statistical computing procedures, quality control, bioassay, survival analysis and introductory stochastic processes will be covered. This course is intended for statistics students and interested students from other disciplines who have appropriate previous courses in statistics. Information on particular offerings will be available at the beginning of each academic year.

STAT*4340.title: Statistical Inference
STAT*4340.description: This course reviews and extends the theory of estimation introduced in STAT*3110. Topics including point estimation, interval estimation, hypothesis testing and decision theory will be presented from both the frequentist and likelihood-based perspectives. Foundational issues concerning the frequentist and Bayesian paradigms will also be discussed.

STAT*4350.title: Applied Multivariate Statistical Methods
STAT*4350.description: This course introduces the multivariate normal, and Wishart and Hotelling's T-square distributions. Topics covered include: statistical inference on the mean vector, canonical correlation, multivariate analysis of variance and covariance, multivariate regression, principal components analysis, and factor analysis. Topics will be illustrated using examples from various disciplines.

STAT*4360.title: Applied Time Series Analysis
STAT*4360.description: This course will investigate the nature of stationary stochastic processes from the spectral and time domain points of view. Aspects of parameter estimation and prediction in a computationally intensive environment will be the presentation style. The methods developed in this course will have applicability in many sciences such as engineering, environmental sciences, geography, soil sciences, and life sciences.

STAT*4600.title: Advanced Research Project in Statistics
STAT*4600.description: Each student in this course will undertake an individual research project in some area of statistics, under the supervision of a faculty member. A written report and a public presentation of the project will be required. Students interested in taking this course must contact the course coordinator at least one month in advance of the first class day of the semester.

prerequisites.raw: 1.00 credits in Statistics at the 3000 level or above.

STAT*4600.restriction: Approval of a supervisor and the course coordinator.

STAT*4820.title: Generalized Linear Models and Extensions
STAT*4820.description: Topics include: generalized linear models; generalized linear mixed models; joint modelling of mean and dispersion; generalized estimating equations; modelling longitudinal categorical data; modelling clustered data. This course will focus both on theory and implementation using relevant statistical software.
STAT*4820.restriction: Credit may be obtained for only one of STAT*4820 and any of the topics courses (STAT*4050, STAT*4060 or STAT*4150) if the topic was taught as a cross-listed course with STAT*6802.

SXGN*1000.title: Introduction to Sexualities and Genders
SXGN*1000.description: This course introduces students to the vocabulary, methods, and theories behind the study of human sexualities and genders. Working with case studies, this course explores how biological, psychological, sociological, cultural, historical, and political determinants as well as experiences connected to "race," class, and (dis)ability shape understandings of genders and sexualities. Topics covered will include representations of sexualities and genders in the media and popular culture, family and work relationships, histories and philosophies of sexualities and genders, sexualities/genders and the law, political movements, self-expression, and mechanisms for enacting social justice.
SXGN*1000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.

SXGN*1010.title: Sexuality, Gender, Race, and Indigeneity
SXGN*1010.description: This course introduces students to the complex intersections between sexuality, gender, race, and indigeneity. Proceeding through a variety of disciplinary approaches, this course explores the complex concepts of race and indigeneity in both Canadian and international contexts and their connections to sexual and gender justice. Topics covered will include intersectionality, the Cohambee River Collective, Two Spirit Identity, Feminist-Indigenous modalities of leadership, Black activism and the queer and trans rights movements of the 70s and 80s, afrofuturism, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, the RiseUp! and the BlackLivesMatter movements, among others.
SXGN*1010.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.

SXGN*2000.title: Sexualities, Genders, & Social Justice Methods
SXGN*2000.description: This course introduces students to social justice approaches and research methodologies that consider the complexities of gender and sexuality within broader social structures. Content will be drawn from disciplinary perspectives in the social sciences, humanities, and fine arts, as well as from interdisciplinary perspectives in the fields of feminist, queer, critical race, disability, and decolonial studies. The course draws on research methods to understand diverse sexual orientations and gender identities as well as community engagement and advocacy.
SXGN*2000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.
SXGN*2000.location: Guelph Sexualities, Genders and Social Change (SXGN) 1

SXGN*2010.title: Theorising Sexualities and Genders
SXGN*2010.description: This course will provide students with foundational knowledge in contemporary theories relating to sexualities and genders. Content covered will include theories drawn from such areas as queer, queer-of- colour, trans, feminist Marxist/socialist, postmodern and poststructural, critical disability studies & crip theory, feminist standpoint theories, post- feminism, feminist affect theory. Students will explore these topics as they relate to contemporary issues in both local and global contexts.
SXGN*2010.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.

SXGN*3000.title: Social Change: Acts of Resistance
SXGN*3000.description: Activism has been the main driver of social justice movements, which are collective forms of political action that often work outside of established, institutional structures. This course looks at historical and contemporary acts of resistance and the political organizing behind them, with a focus on activist strategies, social justice initiatives, and their underlying theoretical frameworks. Using sexuality and gender as our point of departure, we will investigate the difference between grassroots and mainstream activism, the role of allyship, and the importance of representation and participation in social justice movements.
SXGN*3000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.

SXGN*3010.title: Experiential Learning in Sexualities and Genders: Transforming Community
SXGN*3010.description: In this course, students will conduct research and attend weekly seminars on a selected topic related to sexualities, genders, and social change while simultaneously completing a placement with either a community partner (on- or off-campus) or an ongoing faculty research project. The subject matter of the course will thematically connect the interdisciplinary projects being undertaken by those enrolled. Students wishing to take this course must have their project approved by the SXGN Experiential Learning coordinator prior to the end of the previous semester's course selection period.
SXGN*3010.restriction: Restricted to students in SXGN Major or Minor specializations.
SXGN*3010.location: Guelph 2 Sexualities, Genders and Social Change (SXGN)

SXGN*4000.title: Honours Capstone Project in Community Engagement and Advocacy
SXGN*4000.description: This course employs the theories and methodologies learned throughout the degree in SXGN while engaging students in practical outreach and advocacy activities that will prepare them for work with stakeholders in multiple settings (community, non-profit, NGOs). Students will work throughout the semester with a campus, community advocacy, or not for profit group to design and execute a major community outreach project with the goal of staging a public event at the end of the semester (such as a fundraiser or symposium). The final week of class will be reserved for reflection on lessons learned, what worked, and opportunities for growth.
SXGN*4000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs, specializations, majors or semester levels during certain periods. Please see the department for more information.

SXGN*4010.title: Special Topics: Sexualities, Genders, and Bodies
SXGN*4010.description: Drawing on a thematic approach, this course explores critical theories and methodologies from the fields of Sexualities, Genders, and Bodies. Students will engage with topics and issues drawn from a variety of disciplines including feminist, queer, cultural and literary, performance, critical race, diasporic, Indigenous, post/(de)colonial, and disability theories.

THST*1040.title: Introduction to Performance
THST*1040.description: This course functions as a foundation for the study of theatre, performance, and media, however it is not an acting course. Students will be introduced to the formative terms and theories of these academic disciplines as well as the conceptual and social terms of theatre, performance and media. Students will also engage in workshops connecting various theories of performance to practice and everyday life experiences.
THST*1040.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations during certain periods.
THST*1040.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*1190.title: Acting: Voice, Text, Movement
THST*1190.description: This course introduces students to acting styles and techniques by focusing on how actors use bodies, voices, and movements during live theatrical performances. Students will collaborate in workshop and studios settings to explore, develop, and refine their acting skills. All acting methods in this course are taught for live theatre and performance experiences; the course does not teach on-screen acting methods. Students will also learn to think deeply about characters, playscripts, and performance concepts while also incorporating direction from their director, designers, and/or technicians similar to live theatre and performance in the professional industry.
THST*1190.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*1190.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*1200.title: Languages of Media
THST*1200.description: This introductory course examines perception of media and its creative use where current events, internet, film, and music focus on cultural codes, industries, and audiences who make meaning.
THST*1200.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*1270.title: Theatre Practice Foundations
THST*1270.description: This course introduces students to various aspects of theatre and performance practice and careers. Students will engage in modules covering theatre career trajectories, time management, script analysis, production timelines, critical reflection, professional development, problem solving, and evaluating performance-based experiences.
THST*1270.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2040.title: Acting: Character Development
THST*2040.description: This course explores a range of acting methods and development styles for creating and presenting theatrical characters in dramatic performances. Students will study selected approaches to character development through textual analysis, vocal performance, physical movement, and interactive self-exploration. These methods will be applied in a weekly workshop setting, as students prepare monologues and scene work.
THST*2040.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*2040.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2050.title: Devising
THST*2050.description: This course introduces students to devised theatre practices which lead to the creation of unscripted, original performance. Students will become familiar with and practice different methods for devising, including improvisation and collective creation. Topics may include solo devising, group devising, devising through physicality, devising in site-specific locations and integrating forms of media into devised work.
THST*2050.restriction: Restricted to students in a THST specialization.
THST*2050.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2120.title: Writing for Performance
THST*2120.description: This course is a theoretical and applied study of creative writing for performance.
THST*2120.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2190.title: Production Skills Workshop
THST*2190.description: This course introduces students to various production skills and fundamental techniques of theatre including stage management, lighting, sound, set construction, design, drafting, costumes, make-up, properties, cues, and working in a production space. Students in the course may undertake production roles to support a school performance during the term.
THST*2190.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*2190.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2270.title: Theatre Research Skills
THST*2270.description: This course introduces students to the scholarly skills of conducting academic research. Students will engage in modules covering libraries, special collections, archives, academic databases, theatre reviews, interviews, citation styles, theatrical journals, writing styles/expectations, and digital literacy.
THST*2270.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*2270.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2450.title: Approaches to Media Studies
THST*2450.description: This course examines approaches to communication, including cybernetic, anthropologic, semiotic, sensory and other perspectives, focusing on human experiences of media, embodiment, and meaning making.
THST*2450.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*2500.title: Contemporary Cinema
THST*2500.description: Students will review contemporary cinematic expression through curated film and media texts.
THST*2500.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3000.title: Experiential Learning Project
THST*3000.description: Under the supervision of a faculty member, students in this course will be placed in experiential learning situations at the university or in the wider community where they will support a community, academic and/ or creative project. Students will be required to become familiar with the goals and procedures of the project, to apply skills gained in Theatre Studies to the project, and to critically reflect on their experience.
THST*3000.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST). Successful application to the School is required.
THST*3000.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3010.title: Independent Experiential Learning
THST*3010.description: This course is an independent experiential learning option taken by students in consultation with a faculty advisor. Students will apply skills gained in Theatre Studies, including critical reflection, to a project that involves professional-level skills in performance and/or pedagogy. Often these projects are public-facing and community engaged.
THST*3010.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST). Successful application to the School is required.
THST*3010.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3050.title: Devising
THST*3050.description: This course introduces students to devised theatre practices to support the creation of original pieces of theatre and performance. Students will become familiar with and practice different methods for devising, including improvisation and collective creation. Topics may include solo devising, group devising, devising through physicality, devising in site- specific locations and integrating forms of digital media into original devised work.
THST*3050.restriction: THST*2050. Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3050.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3140.title: Performance and the Past
THST*3140.description: This variable content course focuses on a selected area of theatre history. Students will encounter different ways plays, playwrights, and performances have challenged and engaged with major social, political, and cultural issues or movements happening throughout a focal point in global history. Students will read a variety of diverse texts and engage in discussions of their content.
THST*3140.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3150.title: Special Topics: Performance
THST*3150.description: This course provides students with the opportunity to work on projects in devising, acting, playwriting, voice, movement, and directing. The material and topic will vary depending on the instructor.
THST*3150.restriction: THST*3630. Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3150.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3160.title: Special Topics: Production
THST*3160.description: This course provides students with the opportunity to work on projects in scenography, lighting, sound, puppetry, technology and digital multimedia. The material and topic will vary depending on the instructor.
THST*3160.restriction: THST*3630. Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3160.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3170.title: Special Topics: Research
THST*3170.description: This course provides students with the opportunity to work on projects in critical writing, academic research, theatre historiography, and performance theory. The material and topic will vary depending on the instructor.
THST*3170.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3170.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3190.title: Production Workshop
THST*3190.description: Students in this course will undertake the design, technical production and performance of a fully realized theatrical production. All production assignments (cast, technical crews, front of house, dramaturgy, assistant director, assistant designer) will be drawn from students enrolled in the course. The course will be taught as a workshop with skill-specific modules, out-of-class rehearsals, and technical training crew calls.
THST*3190.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3190.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3260.title: Shakespeare in Performance
THST*3260.description: This intensive seminar offers an analysis of a selection of plays by William Shakespeare. In addition to textual analysis, the students will also be introduced to the stage history of the plays being considered, paying attention to the conditions of production. The course includes consideration of plays by Shakespeare currently on stage at the Stratford Festival; this two-week course is taught off-site in Stratford, Ontario. Students will have the opportunity to see these plays (for an additional fee) over the course of the seminar. Please contact the School for further information.

prerequisites.raw: 2.50 credits in Theatre Studies including THST*3140 or 1.00 credits in English

THST*3260.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3340.title: Voice and Text in Performance
THST*3340.description: Within a studio context, students explore acting techniques and exercises to develop their stage voices with a particular focus on Shakespeare's plays as well as performing Shakespearean verse. The course includes consideration of plays by Shakespeare currently on stage at the Stratford Festival; this two-week course is taught off-site in Stratford, Ontario. Students will have the opportunity to see these plays (for an additional fee) over the course of the seminar. Please contact the School for further information.
THST*3340.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3500.title: Experimental Performance Lab
THST*3500.description: Students will collaborate in the exploration of theory and creative practice in an experimental project-based course. Projects may be theatrical or media-based, or an integration of both depending on the iteration of the course. Students will have the opportunity to showcase their experimental work to the public over the course of the term.
THST*3500.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST).
THST*3500.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3520.title: Canadian Theatre
THST*3520.description: This course provides an overview and understanding of Canadian theatre history, playwrights, companies, and productions. Students will encounter Indigenous as well as colonial/settler performance histories throughout Canada with special focus on geographical areas, specific communities, and performance styles/traditions as selected by the course director. Special attention will be placed on representations of diversity, equity, and inclusion--including the lack of these concepts--within Canadian theatre throughout the country.

prerequisites.raw: 8.00 credits including 1.00 credits in Theatre Studies

THST*3520.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3530.title: Canadian Cinema
THST*3530.description: This course teaches students about Canadian films, film history, industries, and directors/filmmakers.
THST*3530.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*3600.title: Directed Reading
THST*3600.description: This course provides independent study opportunities based upon a research project proposal and works cited approved by the Theatre Curriculum committee. Students should seek out an instructor to agree to supervise the course prior to making an application for Directed Reading. Weekly meetings with an instructor will be arranged dependent on the topic or materials being studied.

prerequisites.raw: 3.00 credits in Theatre Studies including THST*3140

THST*3600.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST). Successful application to the Theatre Studies Curriculum Committee required.
THST*3600.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*4190.title: Advanced Production Workshop
THST*4190.description: This course is an intensive study of the methods of theatrical production, organized around a creative project. Students will focus on skill development in the areas of acting, design, costumes, sets, lights, sound, media, and stage management. Students in the course may undertake performance or production roles in a public performance.
THST*4190.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST) with an average of 70% in the specialization.
THST*4190.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*4270.title: Advanced Seminar in Theatre Research
THST*4270.description: This advanced research writing seminar explores critical theory, performance studies, and theatre historiography in relationship to variable theatre and performance content based on the expertise of the course director. Students will review a variety of contemporary theatre and performance research texts and engage with their content.
THST*4270.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST) with an average of 70% in the specialization.
THST*4270.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*4280.title: Ensemble Project
THST*4280.description: Students will engage throughout the semester with the processes of forming a theatre company, theorizing its organizational structure, exploring fundraising and publicity exercises, casting, designing, dramaturging, directing, and mounting a production, and engaging in post-production analysis that assesses the social and theatrical impact of the decisions taken and procedures employed.

prerequisites.raw: THST*2190, (THST*2050 or THST*3050), 2.00 credits at the 3000-level in Theatre Studies

THST*4280.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST), who have completed 7.00 credits in Theatre Studies, and an average of 70% in the specialization.
THST*4280.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*4290.title: Performance Research Seminar
THST*4290.description: This is an advanced research seminar in selected topics in performance studies, theory, practice, and media. The course features variable content. Please consult the School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing's website for more information.
THST*4290.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST) with an average of 70% in the specialization.
THST*4290.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

THST*4650.title: Honours Essay
THST*4650.description: The completion, under direction, of a scholarly essay in the study of drama and/or theatre. Admission is by application to the School with support from the faculty member who will serve as instructor for this honours essay.
THST*4650.restriction: Restricted to students in a Theatre Studies specialization (including BCA.THST). Successful application to School is required.
THST*4650.department: School of Theatre, English, and Creative Writing

TOX*2000.title: Principles of Toxicology
TOX*2000.description: This course will establish the scientific principles underlying the toxic actions of various substances and will introduce the various challenges within the field of toxicology. The chemical nature of injurious substances, their uptake and metabolism by non-target organisms, and their mode of toxic action will be studied in addition to the methods used in safety evaluations and risk assessment.
TOX*2000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course with enrolment initially restricted to students in BSCH.BTOX, BSCH.BPCH, BSES, and BIESP. Please contact the Department for more information.

TOX*3360.title: Environmental Chemistry and Toxicology
TOX*3360.description: This course examines the chemistry of the natural environment and the influence of pollutants upon the environment. Topics will include methods of introduction of pollutants to, and removal of pollutants from, the environment. (Also listed as CHEM*3360.)

TOX*4000.title: Medical Toxicology
TOX*4000.description: This course will focus on the toxicology of mammalian body systems with emphasis on dose-response, mechanisms and the sites of action of major groups of chemical toxicants and biological toxins. The course is designed for students majoring in Bio-Medical Sciences, Biomedical Toxicology and Toxicology.
TOX*4000.restriction: This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to particular programs or specializations. See department for more information.

TOX*4100.title: Toxicological Pathology
TOX*4100.description: Evaluation of the pathologic responses of cells and tissues to toxic compounds. The course is designed for students majoring in toxicology.

TOX*4200.title: Topics in Toxicology
TOX*4200.description: Topics in toxicology will consist of oral and written presentations by students, faculty members, and guest lecturers. The emphasis will be on the broad integrative aspects of toxicology with particular reference to the whole organism and higher levels of natural systems; risk assessment and regulatory toxicology.
TOX*4200.restriction: Restricted to students in BSCH.BTOX, BSCH.BTOX:C

TOX*4590.title: Biochemical Toxicology
TOX*4590.description: This course will focus on the biotransformation of drugs, carcinogens, and other toxicants, including consideration of human health implications of these metabolic processes. The enzymes catalyzing these reactions will be discussed in detail.

TOX*4900.title: Toxicology Research Project I
TOX*4900.description: This research project in toxicology is designed to provide senior undergraduate students with an opportunity to conduct research in an area of toxicology. Students should note that most projects are of two semesters' duration, and should plan their studies with the expectation that they will also register in TOX*4910 in a subsequent semester. Students must make arrangements with both the faculty supervisor and the course coordinator at least one semester in advance. The project supervisor should normally be a faculty member from the Toxicology Program.
TOX*4900.restriction: Normally a minimum cumulative average of 70% is required. Instructor consent required.

TOX*4910.title: Toxicology Research Project II
TOX*4910.description: This course involves a research project in toxicology. Students must make arrangements with both the faculty supervisor and the course coordinator at least one semester in advance. The project supervisor should normally be a faculty member from the Toxicology Program.
TOX*4910.restriction: Normally a minimum cumulative average of 70% is required. Instructor consent required.

UNIV*1000.title: University Learning Skills
UNIV*1000.description: This course will introduce students to learning theory as a foundation for success for university study. The course provides an overview of learning and developmental theories, metacognitive strategies and problem-solving. Students will connect theory to practical experiences that promote self-efficacy and resilience.

UNIV*1200.title: First Year Seminar
UNIV*1200.description: First Year Seminars are interdisciplinary courses designed especially for first year students. The goal of the First Year Seminar course is to provide opportunities for students to participate in small enrolment, discussion-oriented classes in their first year. Different seminar topics are offered each year in each separate course section, reflecting the particular research or professional interest of the course instructor. The seminar course counts as a free elective in the student's Schedule of Studies. For information about how the seminar courses may in some cases fulfil particular program distribution requirements, students should contact their Academic Advisor. A list of current and archived offerings is available by following the links on the First Year Seminars web page.

UNIV*1250.title: Experiential Learning Opportunity I: Peer Helper
UNIV*1250.description: Open only to students who have been accepted into the Peer Helper program. This 0.00 credit course recognizes the successful completion of the Peer Helper training program and a supervised field experience in an approved Peer Helper placement. A pass/fail grade will not be assigned to UNIV*1250 until UNIV*1260 has been completed. Students who wish to enroll in this course should see Student Life and Career Services for more information.
UNIV*1250.restriction: Instructor consent required. Acceptance in the Peer Helper program.

UNIV*1260.title: Experiential Learning Opportunity II: Peer Helper
UNIV*1260.description: Open only to students who have been accepted into the Peer Helper program. This 0.00 credit course recognizes the successful completion of the Peer Helper training program and a supervised field experience in an approved Peer Helper placement. A pass/fail grade will be assigned at the end of the completion of both UNIV*1250 and UNIV*1260. Student who wish to enroll in this course should see Student Life for more information.

UNIV*1300.title: Digital Wisdom: How to Use AI Critically and Responsibly
UNIV*1300.description: This foundational course introduces students to artificial intelligence with a focus on Large Language Models (LLMs), emphasizing practical applications, critical thinking, and ethical considerations. Designed for university students across all disciplines, the course requires no prior technical knowledge.

UNIV*2010.title: Anti-Discrimination and Anti-Oppression
UNIV*2010.description: This course engages students in an introductory discussion of anti- discrimination and anti-oppression principles. Students will be introduced to the historical and lived realities of discrimination that equity-seeking groups have encountered and continue to encounter in Canadian society. In so doing, the course explores social, philosophical, and legal responses to discrimination. Students will also consider social theories of anti-oppression, established principles of intersectional allyship, and strategies to foster and support inclusive spaces.

UNIV*2020.title: Pandemics: Culture, Science, and Society
UNIV*2020.description: This course introduces students to interdisciplinary perspectives on the implications of pandemics, past and present. Drawing on expertise from across the university, the course is structured around a series of webinar panels and seminar discussions. Topics to be explored include pandemics in history, COVID-19 and ethics, COVID-19 and technology, knowledge, misinformation and discourse around pandemics, and the implications of COVID-19 on institutions, work, food, community, resilience.
UNIV*2020.department: Dean's Office, College of Arts, Dean's Office, College of Biological Science, Dean's Office, College of Engineering and Physical Sciences, Dean's Office, College of Social and Applied Human Sciences

UNIV*2100.title: Career Ready: Develop Your Future
UNIV*2100.description: This course introduces students to career development theory and provides the practical knowledge required to succeed in the current and future world of work. Students will examine key concepts from career development theory and will engage in reflective and experiential learning activities aimed at preparing them for job search and the world of work. Within the context of career development, issues related to mental well- being, diversity, intercultural competencies, and the implications of disclosure and accommodation will be studied.
UNIV*2100.restriction: UNIV*3140. This is a Priority Access Course. Enrolment may be restricted to non-BCOMM and non-Co-op students.

UNIV*2200.title: Towards Sustainability
UNIV*2200.description: This course will introduce students to the complex and interrelated issues of sustainability. It will explore why it is important to understand the environmental, economic and social issues of our world and why they must be addressed in a holistic way. It will allow students to realize the impact they will make on the world and give them to knowledge to develop values that will help chart their career. This course will provide the opportunity to understand issues beyond their chosen discipline.

UNIV*2250.title: Experiential Learning Opportunity III: Peer Helper
UNIV*2250.description: Open only to students who have been accepted into the Peer Helper program. This 0.00 credit course recognizes the successful completion of the Peer Helper training program and a supervised field experience in an approved Peer Helper placement. A pass/fail grade will not be assigned to UNIV*2250 until UNIV*2260 has been completed. Students who wish to enroll in this course should see Student Life and Career Services for more information.

UNIV*2260.title: Experiential Learning Opportunity IV: Peer Helper
UNIV*2260.description: Open only to students who have been accepted into the Peer Helper program. This 0.00 credit course recognizes the successful completion of the Peer Helper training program and a supervised field experience in an approved Peer Helper placement. A pass/fail will be assigned at the end of the completion of both UNIV*2250 and UNIV*2260. Students who wish to enroll in this course should see Student Life and Career Services for more information.

UNIV*2800.title: Independent Research
UNIV*2800.description: This course offers students in any program the opportunity to pursue credit for relevant research experience (paid or unpaid) with the addition of course assignments either during the research experience or in a following semester. Details of the learning outcomes and requirements will be outlined in a learning contract initiated by the student and approved by a faculty supervisor. Open to students in any undergraduate program. Students should consult their Academic Advisor to determine how the course will count toward their program. Please consult the website for more information.

UNIV*3000.title: Civic Engagement & Service Learning
UNIV*3000.description: This course provides a unique opportunity for students to develop civic leadership skills and increase their awareness and appreciation for the social relevance of higher education through a community service- learning experience. Curricular and co-curricular learning are integrated through continued academic study and its application, modification, and critique in a community context. Students will conduct research and seminars on a selected topic while simultaneously completing a placement in a community agency appropriate to that topic.

UNIV*3800.title: Independent Research
